definition: The process of keeping financial records. Helps businesses control finances, make decisions, and report to stakeholders. Primary purpose: to accurately record and maintain all financial transactions.
Records past transactions and reports to outside parties (investors, lenders, regulators). Historical and external.

Reports internally to management to help plan and make future decisions. Future-focused and internal — does not have to follow GAAP. Managerial accountants accumulate data on costs and profits to support management.
Generally Accepted Accounting Principles — the standard rules U.S. companies use for financial reporting.
Software that collects, stores, manages, processes, and reports financial data. Helps a company's different departments work together.
A system that centralizes financial data and leads to better decision-making.
A snapshot of the company's financial position at one point in time. Equation: Assets = Liabilities + Owner's Equity.
Shows revenues minus expenses over a time period, resulting in net income or net loss.
Shows money flowing in and out of the business — operating, investing, and financing activities.
Shows how the owner's retained earnings changed due to profits, dividend payments, and other adjustments.
Processes a business uses to ensure the reliability of financial records and compliance with laws.
Find errors or fraud after they occur. Example: annual audit.
Stop errors before they happen. Example: segregation of duties, dual authorization for checks.
Fix problems once discovered. Example: recovering stolen data, restoring backups.

Divides financial responsibilities among different people so no single person has too much control or power. Most important internal control — prevents fraud. Also called segregation of control on boards of directors.
An independent verification of a company's financial records. Virtual audits share documents online and are more efficient than traditional in-person audits.
Measure ability to pay short-term obligations. Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities.
Measure how well a business generates profit. Examples: profit margin, return on equity (ROE).
Measure how efficiently a business uses assets to generate revenue.
Measure how much debt a company uses relative to equity.
Price per share divided by earnings per share. Common heading on stock tables. Shows how much investors pay per dollar of earnings.
Shares of ownership in a corporation. Stockholders share in profits (dividends) and bear risk.
Most basic form of ownership; holders can vote but are last to receive assets if the company fails.
Holders receive dividends before common stockholders and have priority in liquidation, but usually cannot vote.
A portion of profits distributed to shareholders; decided by the board of directors.
Stock prices are rising; investor confidence is high.
Stock prices are falling.
Trading stock using material, non-public information. Illegal — can result in fines or jail time.
52-week high and low, stock symbol, dividend, P/E ratio, volume, and closing price.

Represents the lowest and highest price at which a stock has been traded during the past year. Helps investors determine whether the current price is a good time to buy or sell based on historical price range.
A well-regarded publication providing detailed reports on approximately 1,700 stocks. One of the most trusted stock information resources.

An investment scam mostly conducted online. Scammers buy a small or micro-cap stock, then hype it up on the internet with false information to drive up the price. Once the price rises, the scammers sell their shares, leaving other investors with worthless stock. Different from a Ponzi scheme, which uses new investor money to pay old investors.

The area of a company responsible for managing capital structure, budgeting, financial modeling and planning, funding, dividends, and taxation. Example: someone who prepares bills and invoices is working in corporate finance.
A debt instrument where the investor lends money to an issuer (government or corporation) in exchange for interest payments and return of principal at maturity.
The return earned on a bond. Yields typically increase during economic expansion as interest rates rise.
Where long-term securities (stocks, bonds due in more than 1 year) are traded.
Where short-term debt securities (less than 1 year) are traded.
A pool of money collected from many investors and invested in a diversified portfolio of stocks, bonds, or other securities. Managed by professional fund managers.
A pool of funds donated to nonprofits (universities, charities) to invest and generate income. Organizations like universities often use endowment funds to invest money obtained from donors.
A pooled investment fund that uses aggressive strategies (leverage, short selling, derivatives) to maximize returns. Available only to a limited number of private investors.
Financial instruments whose value depends on the value of other financial instruments or market indices. Often used by investors to hedge (reduce) risk. Example: options and futures contracts.
Helps firms raise funds by issuing securities in the form of stocks or bonds. Locates outside sources of money for businesses.
Pools funds from investors and invests them in securities based on investor needs. Primary goal: to pool and invest funds on behalf of investors.
Limits the total amount of money that may be owed and charges interest on outstanding balances. Customers who don't pay in full each month are charged interest. Example: credit cards.
The customer makes a down payment, then a series of regular payments for a set period. Used for big-ticket items like furniture and cars. Example: car loan.
Allows credit users to buy at any time during a set period (usually 30 days) and pay the full amount at the end of the period. No interest if paid on time.
A rating assigned by credit bureaus to evaluate creditworthiness. Used by financial institutions when deciding whether to approve loans. Higher scores = better terms.
A financial cooperative that provides savings and credit services to its members, including small loans, installment credit, and low interest rates.
A full-service bank offering checking and savings accounts, secured and unsecured loans, installment plans, and more.
A government institution that manages a country's money supply and interest rates. If the economy is sluggish, a central bank may lower interest rates to encourage spending and investing.
Provides a wide variety of financial services; supervised by the Federal Reserve Board.
A financial intermediary that facilitates buying and selling of securities for clients.
Middlemen (e.g., commercial banks, brokerage firms, insurance institutions, financial-planning firms) that move financial products between producers and end users.
A type of insurance company owned by its members (stockholders). Members retain and control their own liability risk.
The profit percentage without adjusting for inflation.
The profit percentage adjusted for inflation and taxes. More accurate measure of actual purchasing power gained.
Spreading investments across different asset classes or sectors to reduce risk. The key strategy for managing investment risk.
An investor's tendency to prefer domestic investments over international ones. Reduces diversification. Often caused by higher perceived risk with international investing.
Evaluating a stock by analyzing financial statements, management quality, and economic factors.
Evaluating a stock by studying price charts and trading volume patterns.
An investment fraud that pays early investors using funds from new investors. Promises high returns with little risk. Always eventually collapses.
FV = PV x (1 + r)^n. Example: $500 invested at 5% for 1 year = $500 x 1.05 = $525.
PV = FV / (1 + r/n)^(n*t). Example: To have $600 in 2 years at ~3%, invest about $565 today.
A financial plan that estimates future income and expenses. A good budget is realistic, flexible, and aligned with company goals. Money is a limited resource — budgets must prioritize.
Determine the likely amount of available funding before projecting expenses.
The difference between a budgeted amount and the actual result. Budgeted - Actual = Variance.
Investigating differences between planned and actual outcomes. Focuses on three productive inputs: materials, labor, and overhead.
Difference between the standard price and actual price paid for raw materials.
Comparing additional (marginal) benefits to additional costs of an action. Goal: ensure marginal revenue equals or exceeds marginal cost.
The additional revenue generated by selling one more unit.
The additional cost of producing one more unit.
Money is accepted in exchange for goods and services — its primary function.
Money can be saved and holds value for future use. Example: putting $100 per paycheck into savings.
Money is used to compare the value of different products and services. Example: comparing the price of two candy bars.

Money that has no intrinsic value of its own. Its value comes entirely from government backing. Examples: paper currency like a $20 bill, the euro, the Indian rupee. Contrast: gold and silver are commodity money because they have intrinsic value. A credit card and a check are not fiat money because their value is not directly backed by the government.
Money that has intrinsic value as a commodity in itself. Examples: gold, silver. Different from fiat money, which has no inherent value.
A record of all checks written and money spent from a checking account. To balance: compare your register to your bank statement and subtract any fees or charges.
A goal achievable within about a year. Example: saving $50 for Christmas presents.
A goal that takes more than a year to achieve. Example: saving for college, buying a car, or getting a job at a publishing company.
Key rule: do not buy more with your credit card than you can afford. Think of a credit card as a loan. Pay on time to build good credit and track your spending to avoid debt.

If struggling to pay bills, contact your creditors early to work out a modified payment plan — they may be willing to help. Once a debt collector is assigned, it is usually too late to renegotiate. Secured loans (tied to a car or home) risk asset repossession if you miss payments.

Money earned through active work — wages, salaries, freelance income, and side-business profits. Example: Amir earns $2,000/month from his drone photography business; this is earned income subject to taxes.
Money earned passively — from interest, dividends, rent, or government transfer payments. Not tied to active labor.
The first price at which a stock was sold on a given trading day when the markets open.
The number of shares of a stock traded in a given day.
Shows the relationship between the price of a stock and the company's earnings per share.
The highest and lowest prices at which the stock has traded over the past year. Helps investors decide if now is a good time to buy or sell.
Shows the annual dividend payment per share. A blank dividend column means the company does not currently pay dividends. A blank does NOT mean zero trades or a negative dividend.
The dollar value change in a stock's price from the previous day's closing price. Shown as positive or negative.
Where companies sell securities to the public for the first time (initial public offerings — IPOs). Companies raise new capital directly from investors.

Where investors trade securities among themselves after the initial offering. Companies are not involved. Examples: New York Stock Exchange, London Stock Exchange, Nasdaq. Also called 'the stock market.'
Offers multiple types of financial products (loans, insurance, trust services) using ONE shared pool of capital. All products come from one entity.
Offers banking products directly to customers AND sells other financial products (especially insurance) through subsidiaries. Each subsidiary has its own separate pool of capital.

A financial analysis technique that reviews each line item on an income statement as a proportion of total revenue. Analysis goes 'up and down' (vertically) the financial document. Useful for comparing profitability across line items.
A financial analysis technique that compares year-over-year (YoY) changes in each line item. Analysis goes 'left and right' (horizontally) across time periods.
Determines the level at which revenues equal total costs — the point where the business neither profits nor loses.

A liquidity ratio that determines whether a company has enough short-term assets to cover its immediate liabilities WITHOUT selling inventory. Excludes inventory because it may not convert to cash quickly. More conservative than the current ratio.
Measures a company's ability to meet current liabilities out of ALL current assets (including inventory). Less strict than the acid-test ratio.
The average number of days it takes a company to collect payment after making a sale.
Only looks at the most liquid assets (cash and cash equivalents) when measuring a company's ability to meet short-term obligations. The most conservative liquidity measure.

A valuation ratio that compares a company's market capitalization to its operating cash flow. Formula: Market Capitalization ÷ Operating Cash Flow. Example: stock at $25/share × 1,000,000 shares = $25M market cap; $15M operating cash flow → ratio = 25 ÷ 15 = 1.67. Used to evaluate whether a stock is over- or under-valued relative to the cash it generates.
The profit earned from the appreciation (increase in value) of an investment. Most governments collect taxes on capital gains.

Money lost from the depreciation (decrease in value) of investments. Capital losses can be subtracted from capital gains to reduce the tax owed. If capital losses exceed capital gains, no capital gains tax is owed.
In the U.S., long-term investments (held more than 1 year) are taxed at a lower capital gains rate than short-term investments. Holding investments longer reduces tax burden.
An annual report filed with the SEC. Includes detailed information about the company, its competitors, risks it faces, legal proceedings, and audited financial statements.
Filed for each of the first three quarters of the fiscal year. Focuses on the company's financial situation for that quarterly period.

Filed when a significant, nonrecurring event occurs before a 10-Q or 10-K is due. Must be filed within 15 days of the event. Examples of triggering events: acquiring another company, key management changes, new product introduction, legal actions.
Tells you when company insiders (owners, directors, key management) are buying or selling the company's stock.

Conducted by an independent third party outside the company. Purpose: prove the accuracy and truthfulness of the company's financial statements. Confirms that financial statements are fairly presented.
Conducted by someone within the company on the company's behalf. Purpose: measure current performance and identify areas for improvement. More operational in focus.

Key capability: creating unlimited 'what-if' scenarios to see the impact of different decisions and develop the most realistic budget. Good apps also maintain an audit trail of all data entries, flag overspent accounts, and retain prior-year budget data for comparison.

Budgets can be described in terms of money, maps, and management. Money = they deal with financial resources. Maps = they provide direction, like a roadmap. Management = they are essential management tools for planning and control.
The difference between a business's current assets and current liabilities. Represents the short-term financial health of the business.
Focuses on the company's current balance of assets and liabilities. Involves managing accounts payable, accounts receivable, inventory, and cash. Short-term in nature (one year or less).
Determine which long-term projects the business will invest in, how the investment will be financed, and whether to pay dividends. Long-term in nature.
The mix of debt and equity financing used to fund investments and projects.
Using financial instruments to manage exposure to market risk (risk of financial loss due to decreased investment value). Long-term in nature.

Uses credit or debit cards stored electronically in a digital wallet. Relies on Radio Frequency Identification (RFID) or Near Field Communication (NFC) technology to make secure payments. Example: tapping a card or phone at checkout.
Technology that uses radio waves to transmit data. Used in contactless payment systems.
A short-range wireless technology enabling communication between devices within a few centimeters. Used in contactless payments and mobile wallets.

A digital or virtual currency that uses cryptography for security. Not tied to any country or central bank — therefore considered decentralized. Examples: Bitcoin, Ethereum. Contrast: the U.S. dollar, euro, and Swiss franc are centralized currencies controlled by governments.
A currency not controlled by any single country or central authority. Cryptocurrencies are decentralized. Traditional national currencies (USD, GBP, EUR) are centralized.

term: Detecting Conflicts of Interest with Financial Analysis
definition: Financial analysis applications can compare employee phone numbers and addresses to vendor payables information. Matching data between employees and vendors can reveal potential conflicts of interest (e.g., an employee approving payments to a company they own).

Ensuring that a company's financial resources are allocated appropriately and ethically to fulfill the company's mission. Example: a company that spends heavily on advertising while neglecting quality control is failing at financial stewardship.

Dividing work so each worker performs a specific task. Increases efficiency and productivity. Types: by task (worker always does one job), by time (worker covers specific hours), by seniority (senior workers choose shifts).
A worker is assigned one specific job function and performs it exclusively. Example: one worker makes ice cream; another works the counter full time.

Employers divide cash-related responsibilities among different employees so that one person's records are checked by another. This reduces the chance that a dishonest employee can steal by altering cash records. Also: train staff on prices (reduces price-ticket switching), provide clear policies on office supply use (reduces pilferage).

Planning (deciding what will be done and how), Organizing (dividing work among the organization), Staffing (finding qualified employees), Directing (providing guidance to workers), Controlling (monitoring performance and correcting problems).
The management function that monitors work effort, evaluates performance, and corrects problems if necessary.
The management function that provides guidance to workers and work projects so they can help the business meet its goals.
Deciding how the work required by an organization will be divided and structured.
Deciding what will be done in an organization and how it will be accomplished.
Finding qualified employees to perform job tasks.

The process of comparing cash received with receipts or transaction records to ensure they match. Example: at end of day, a business should compare the cash in the register against all sales receipts to verify accuracy.

Operating cash flow minus capital expenditures. Represents the cash a business generates after spending on maintaining or expanding its asset base. Formula: Free Cash Flow = Operating Cash Flow – Capital Expenditures.

A flat tax charged per unit of a product, regardless of location or price. Example: the federal government charges $0.184 per gallon of gasoline regardless of where or at what price it is sold. Different from sales tax (percentage of sale price) or income tax.
Before choosing an investment broker, always run a background check to verify their credentials and history. Do not simply read their advertisements or choose based on family recommendations.
The cumulative gross pay earned from the beginning of the fiscal/calendar year through the most recent pay period. Calculated by adding the current gross pay to the prior YTD gross pay figure.

A professional consulted for advice about tax filing options — such as which deductions to take, how to file, and how to minimize tax liability. Different from a broker (investments) or estate planner (asset transfer).

Raising capital by selling shares (ownership) in the company. Also called equity financing. The business gains funds without taking on debt, but owners dilute their ownership stake. Contrast with debt financing (taking loans).
Entrepreneurs should keep business and personal finances completely separate for transparency, accountability, and accurate reporting. This also protects personal assets and makes accounting easier.

Charitable contributions made by a business or individual can often be deducted from taxable income, reducing the tax burden. Employees who track receipts for charitable gifts can use them for tax deductions.

A type of employer-funded retirement plan that guarantees a specific income to the employee after retirement, usually based on years of service and salary. Also called a defined benefit plan. Different from a 401(k) or IRA (employee-funded, market-dependent).

term: Home Purchase Credit = Savings & Loan Associations
definition: For purchasing a home, savings and loan associations (S&Ls, also called thrift institutions) are traditionally the best-fit source of credit. They specialize in mortgage lending. Commercial banks and credit unions also lend for home purchases, but S&Ls were historically designed specifically for this purpose.

A budget that is continuously updated by adding a new period (such as a month or quarter) as the most recent one ends, so there is always a set future period budgeted. Advantage: encourages managers to assess activities and adjust financial figures more frequently than a static annual budget.

The finance department's primary responsibility is to ensure that money is available when the business needs it — securing funding for capital expenditures like new buildings and equipment, and managing cash flow so operations are never short on funds.

A budgeting method in which the budget is rebuilt from zero at the beginning of each new budgeting period. Every expense must be justified anew, regardless of prior-year spending. Contrast with incremental budgeting (adjusting previous year's budget by a percentage).

term: Cost-Benefit Analysis — Increased Productivity as Benefit
definition: When conducting a cost-benefit analysis, a business lists both costs (increased expenses, decreased efficiency) and benefits (increased productivity, market share, revenue). Increased productivity is a tangible benefit that can offset costs.

term: Comparing Financial Info Across Time Periods
definition: Businesses often compare financial information from different time periods to detect trends — spotting patterns in revenue, expenses, or profitability over months or years. This is a core purpose of financial analysis.

Total earnings before any deductions (taxes, benefits, etc.) are taken out. The 'gross' amount on a pay stub reflects what you earned before deductions. Net income is what you take home after deductions.

term: Credit Report — Report Suspicious Activity Immediately
definition: When checking your personal credit report for accuracy, you should report any suspicious activity (unknown accounts, fraudulent charges) immediately. Do NOT ignore accounts you don't recognize — this could indicate identity theft.

Full-service investment brokers charge the highest commissions of any broker type because they provide personalized advice, research, and portfolio management. Discount and online brokers charge lower commissions but provide fewer services.

An arrangement that allows a customer to purchase and use goods or services now and pay for them at a future time. The customer receives the good immediately but payment is deferred. Different from layaway (pay first, receive later) or cash sale (pay and receive simultaneously).

term: Track and Record Expenses for Personal Budget
definition: To develop a realistic personal budget, you must track and record your actual expenses over time. This reveals true spending patterns and prevents underestimating costs — the most common reason budgets fail.

A savings reserve equivalent to 3–6 months of living expenses. Protects against catastrophic events such as job loss, health emergencies, or major unexpected expenses. This is an important financial goal — not a fund for vacations or clothing.

In a cost-benefit analysis, intangible items (such as improved morale, brand reputation, or goodwill) are difficult or impossible to measure in exact dollar amounts. Recognizing intangible costs and benefits gives a more complete picture of a decision's true impact.

The process of deciding which long-term capital investment projects a business should undertake. Managers estimate each potential project's value to the business and compare options to determine the best use of capital funds. Example: a finance manager evaluating which project to invest in by estimating each one's value.

When evaluating the quality and reliability of an online source, the most important factor is the reputable credentials of the author — their expertise, qualifications, and organizational affiliation. Recency, URL type (.com vs .gov), and lack of advertising are secondary considerations.

When a business attempts to reduce costs, it typically starts by cutting variable costs (which fluctuate with production/sales volume) before fixed costs. Variable costs are easier to reduce without disrupting core operations.

Businesses should periodically evaluate their relationships with banks to make sure the bank understands the business's needs, goals, and industry — not just whether they will lower interest rates. A bank that truly understands the business is a better long-term partner.

term: Impulsive Spending = Barrier to Financial Goals
definition: Impulsive spending — buying things on a whim rather than according to a budget — is one of the most common barriers to reaching financial saving goals. Avoiding impulse purchases is essential for building savings and achieving financial targets.
Costs that do not change with changes in sales volume. Examples: insurance, rent, taxes.
Costs that change as sales volume changes. Examples: raw materials, hourly wages, sales commissions.
Costs directly traceable to a specific product, project, or department. Examples: raw materials, direct labor.
Costs that cannot be directly linked to one cost object; shared across multiple areas. Examples: rent, insurance, utilities, electricity in a factory.
Costs that must be paid now or in the future using resources not yet spent.
Costs already paid and unrecoverable. Should NOT influence future decisions.
Splitting costs among departments, products, or regions within a business. Purpose: measure costs of inventory and cost of goods sold, and identify true profitability.
A variable that causally affects costs. Example: the number of customers drives internet service costs.
Anything to which costs are assigned — a product, service, department, or project.
A tax on the transfer of assets from a deceased person to a beneficiary.
A tax on non-essential, high-end goods. Examples: mansions, yachts, expensive jewelry.
A tax on earned profit. Businesses pay a percentage of their profits to the government.
A tax on real estate and other property, assessed by local governments.
A tax on business profits earned by corporations.
A tax on goods brought across international borders. Controls the flow of goods in and out of a country.
Financial aid provided by the government to individuals or businesses. Types include grants (free money), loans, and tax breaks.
Collects, stores, manages, processes, and reports financial data. Helps departments work together.
Integrates all business processes and data into one system. Frequently used as a source for master data management.
A singular storage location for all organizational data. Provides up-to-date data and consistency.
Handles routine daily transactions at the operational level. Examples: payroll, hotel reservations, sales orders.
A production practice focused on eliminating waste and creating more value with fewer resources. Increases speed and productivity.

A data-driven strategy to reduce defects and variation in processes. In finance, Six Sigma helps sustain regulatory compliance and close control loops. Lessens inefficiencies and identifies problem areas.
A company's agreement with customers that it will consistently meet their expectations. Must be consistently delivered by each employee.
AI reduces human error in compliance functions, increases efficiency and effectiveness, and lowers costs. A key benefit: reduces human error.
The process of transforming information into a secret code so only authorized people can read it. Used to protect confidential financial data transmitted over the internet or stored on servers.
Security systems that prevent unauthorized access to computer networks. Help protect against identity theft and data breaches.
A business's internal computer network. Used to exchange information among employees while keeping confidential data protected from outsiders.
Fulfilling the requirements of the law. Five areas: identification, prevention, monitoring, resolution, and advisory.
Failing to fulfill compliance requirements. Consequences in finance: fines, damaged reputation, and loss of clients.
The literal meaning of a law as written, word for word.
The intent behind a law; what lawmakers meant to achieve.
An audit conducted online by sharing documents digitally. Same goal as a traditional audit but more efficient.
A request for information, appointments, or assistance. Must be as specific as possible and include a specific request in every type of inquiry letter.
A message that provides facts or data to the recipient. Example: a letter stating that a company is offering discounts.
Using an authoritative source (credentials, expertise) to convince an audience. Example: a doctor recommending a product gains credibility from her title and degree.
In their raw state, financial data are not very useful to businesses. Data must be gathered and organized in a meaningful way before they can support decision-making.

A project planning document that includes choosing and implementing standards for how quality will be measured. Without quality standards, it is difficult to know whether the project has met expectations. (Scheduling, changes, and risks are covered in other parts of the project plan.)

A legal charge that applies to individuals or businesses that deliberately conceal important information from law enforcement agencies. Example: an accounting firm hiding a client's questionable financial data from investigators. Can result in fines and imprisonment for the employees involved.

Mergers and acquisitions commonly cause disparate (incompatible) data structures within large corporations' financial-information management systems. Even when two companies merge, their historical financial data may be housed in different formats, making data integration difficult. Channels of distribution, capital structure, and accounting methods do not commonly cause this problem.
Risk from within the organization. Examples: injured employees, employee theft, dishonest employees, equipment failure.
Risk from outside the organization. Examples: changes in import laws, economic downturns, competitor actions, natural disasters, inflation.

An external risk that occurs when a country's government changes its policies — such as enacting trade barriers, tariffs, or new regulations — in ways that impact companies doing business there. Businesses cannot control political risk. Example: a government enacts a trade barrier that restricts imports, directly impacting foreign companies.
A risk that meets criteria for insurance coverage — measurable, accidental, not catastrophic, and shared by many.
A risk too unpredictable for insurance companies to cover. Examples: customer buying habits, tax increases, general business risk.
Risk where the only outcome is loss (no chance of gain). Usually insurable. Examples: fire, theft, accidents.
Risk where there is a possibility of gain or loss. Usually not insurable. Examples: investing in stocks, launching a new product.

Potential events or situations that can cause injury or harm to people, property, or the environment. Most hazard risks can only cause losses, not gains. Examples: natural disasters (tornadoes, earthquakes, floods, blizzards, hurricanes, wildfires), fires, accidents. Usually insurable.

Possible events and situations that can result from employee actions, core processes, and daily business activities. Threatens the day-to-day activities required for a company to operate. Example: a flood damages a company's inventory warehouse; an employee makes a processing error.

Concerns the overall business environment, economic or political issues, or the company's long-term objectives. Examples: a competitor launches a superior product; a company's strategy fails to account for market shifts.
Possible events or situations that directly influence a company's cash flow. Examples: interest rate changes, credit risk, currency fluctuation.
Eliminate the activity that causes the risk. Best for high-probability, high-impact risks.
Take steps to lower likelihood or impact. Examples: safety training, fire drills, equipment inspections.
Shift the risk to another party. Most common method: purchase insurance.
Accept the risk and prepare to absorb the loss. Used when the cost of other responses is too high.

A good decision maker will sometimes accept the risks associated with a decision because they will be able to reach an important goal. The benefits of achieving the goal outweigh the potential cost of the risks. Decision makers use a systematic process to evaluate risks — all risks have potential negative impact, but accepting them can be justified when the goal is valuable enough.
A comprehensive, firmwide, top-down business strategy to limit company losses. Steps: objective setting, risk assessment, risk response, and monitoring/reporting.

term: First Task in Creating a Risk-Management Plan
definition: The first task is identifying as many known risks as possible. After that, project managers determine how to avoid those risks or limit their potential impact. Figuring out how team members will communicate is a task for the communications plan, not the risk-management plan.
A tool that inputs data or goals to provide risk predictions for a given strategy or initiative.
A comprehensive technology system to collect and communicate risk data. Key functions: monitoring, reporting, and analyzing risk.
Types: Preventative controls (e.g., keypads restricting entry) stop problems before they happen. Detective controls (e.g., audits) find problems after they occur.
Financial protection against specified losses in exchange for premium payments.
The amount paid periodically to maintain insurance coverage.
The amount the policyholder pays out of pocket before insurance pays.
Formed by a corporation or industry association to insure its own risks. Advantages: lower premiums and ability to control the claims process.
Covers the policyholder for a specific time period. Lower cost but no cash value.
Permanent coverage with fixed premiums and cash value that builds over time.
Permanent life insurance that provides lifelong coverage and allows tax-free savings growth. Adaptable to changing needs.
Covers claims made against a business for damages caused to others.
Forming a captive insurance company can reduce, eliminate, or defer federal taxes on a corporation's insurance premiums. It also typically decreases the tax due on a company's loss reserves.
Focuses on stopping certain losses from occurring before they happen. Example: training employees on safe lifting techniques to prevent back injuries.
Lessens the impact of a loss after it occurs. Example: automatic sprinklers in a facility — they won't prevent a fire, but they reduce how much damage it causes.
Choosing not to take part in a risky activity at all. Example: a business deciding not to enter a high-risk market.

A bias is a prejudice you have either for or against something. Bias can limit your ability to assess the long-term impact of a decision by preventing you from considering all outcomes objectively. It is important to acknowledge your biases and not let them control your decisions.

Regularly backing up computer systems and storing the backup information off-site reduces financial losses caused by uncontrollable events such as fire, flood, and electrical outages. Businesses that don't back up regularly may lose data permanently. Backup is different from intrusion detection (which prevents unauthorized access) and encryption (which secures access).

Software that alerts a company to security breaches and other IT threats in real time. Businesses invest in threat prediction software to proactively identify and respond to cybersecurity risks before they cause damage. Different from word processing, presentation, or general risk management software.

When a business purchases an extended warranty on equipment or vehicles, it is transferring risk to the warranty provider. Instead of absorbing potential repair costs itself, it pays a premium to shift that financial risk to another party.

Businesses frequently offer health insurance as part of their employee benefit plan. It is one of the most valued and common benefits, helping attract and retain talent while reducing employee out-of-pocket healthcare costs.
Managing risks that involve potential physical harm. Examples: ensuring employees know where all building exits are located; maintaining clear aisles; installing proper safety equipment; fire drills.
The risk that merchandise will go out of style or become obsolete before it can be sold. A type of economic risk that businesses must anticipate and plan for with inventory management strategies.

Individuals or businesses without adequate insurance face significant risks including possible tax penalties (for not having required coverage), hospitals potentially refusing care, and full financial exposure to losses. Uninsured status does not reduce deductibles or increase premiums — those only apply when you have insurance.

Provides benefits to employees who are injured on the job, covering medical expenses and lost wages during recovery. Required by law in most states. Example: a shoe store employee injured during a shift is covered under workers' compensation.

Covers income losses when an individual is unable to work due to an injury or illness. Helps replace lost wages during recovery. Example: a person who breaks their leg snowboarding and cannot work for three months uses disability insurance to cover living expenses.

The risk that currency value fluctuations in international business will reduce profits. Example: a U.S. company operating in Chile finds that when converting profits back to U.S. dollars, the depreciation of the Chilean peso means they earned less than expected. Also called currency risk.

A detailed record of owned items for insurance purposes. Should include receipts for purchases (for proof of value), family heirlooms, photographs, and items in garages and sheds. Used when filing insurance claims after theft, fire, or disaster.
The system of rules, practices, and processes directing and controlling a company. Primary goal: ensure companies are held to high ethical standards and balance stakeholder desires.
Any person or group with an interest in a company's outcomes — investors, customers, employees, the government.
Elected group of individuals representing shareholders and overseeing company management. Internal members are not paid extra for board work; external (independent) members are typically paid.
A tactic used to encourage employees to help achieve governance objectives by sharing company profits.
Controls that come from within the organization. Examples: board of directors' policies, company's code of conduct, business objectives, separation of duties.
Controls imposed on a company from outside the organization. Example: government regulations. These are external because they originate outside the company and are not self-imposed.
Basic principles that guide behavior; standards of right and wrong in professional conduct.
A formal document outlining a company's ethical standards. In financial institutions: do not share confidential information or use it for personal gain; disclose positive and negative information.
The legal obligation to act in the best interests of another person.
Helps clients make money decisions — execute trades, construct portfolios, and provide financial planning. A true statement: they help clients execute trades in the market.
Ensures a business adheres to external rules and internal controls. Five responsibilities: identification, prevention, monitoring, resolution, and advisory.
A compliance task — creating mechanisms and rules that guide decision-making and prevent violations.
In the finance industry: fines, damaged reputation, audits, and loss of clients.
The concept that the government must respect all legal rights owed to individuals and businesses. Procedural due process requires providing notice before taking action.
Any job involving management of money. Examples: fraud investigator, tax accountant, loan officer, financial planner, compliance officer, auditor.
Your potential for learning a skill; a natural knack for something. Matching a career to your aptitudes makes achieving your goals more realistic.
Provide basic job experience and a starting point for a career. Many fields are competitive — entry-level jobs help build credentials.
Should include education, work experience, and contact information. References are often listed separately.

A document submitted with a resume to introduce yourself, express interest in a position, and summarize why you are a good candidate for the job. Does NOT list references (those go in a separate document) and should not duplicate the resume — critical information missing from the resume should be added to the resume itself.

The most common reason people lose or leave their jobs is difficulty getting along with others — poor interpersonal and communication skills. Not poor job performance, poor self-esteem, or difficulty understanding the job.

A creative problem-solving technique where you first think of ways to make the problem WORSE, then use those negative statements to generate ideas for improving the situation. Helps break through creative blocks by approaching the problem from an opposite angle.

term: Crisis Communication — Don't Refuse to Comment
definition: In a crisis situation, refusing to comment to the press is an example of responding inappropriately. Companies should have a crisis communication plan and address the situation transparently rather than going silent.

Employees should avoid sharing their current salary with coworkers. Salary discussions can create conflict, resentment, and legal issues. This is information to share only with HR or management when relevant.

Responsible employees who look for ways to increase their own productivity cost the business less than irresponsible employees who waste time or resources. Responsible workers arrive on time, follow through, and take ownership of their work.

term: Independent Board Members Provide Objective Perspective
definition: Organizations should include independent members on their board of directors because they provide an objective, outside perspective. They are not part of day-to-day management and are less likely to be influenced by internal politics.

Includes communicating honestly and openly with all stakeholders — employees, investors, customers, suppliers, and the community. Ethical stakeholder management is not just about financial performance but about responsible, transparent relationships.

Business innovation includes introducing more sustainable packaging, processes, or products. Innovation is not limited to technology — it includes any new way of doing something that improves results, including environmental responsibility.
Sent after a meeting, interview, or other communication to give thanks, check in, or inquire about a job application. Demonstrates professionalism.
An element of networking; asking an influential contact to vouch for you to a potential employer.
Connecting with others for professional advancement. Examples: conferences, professional associations, social gatherings.
Groups that represent industries. They influence legislation (lobbying), provide education, and connect professionals.

A program that encourages clients or associates to provide referrals — recommendations of new potential clients. Example: a financial planner implements a referral system, offering a free consultation or discount to clients who refer a new customer. Building a referral system is one of the best ways to build professional relationships in finance. Reading industry trends or printing business cards alone does not build relationships without action.

term: Conflict of Interest Detection with Financial Analysis
definition: Financial analysis applications can identify potential conflicts of interest by comparing employee phone numbers and addresses to vendor information in accounts payable. Matching employee data with vendor data may reveal that an employee is approving payments to a company they are connected to. This is different from comparing inventory counts, purchase orders, or employee termination records.

term: When to Send a Follow-Up Letter
definition: Send a follow-up letter even if you are no longer interested in the job. This is courteous, builds a positive relationship, and keeps the door open for future opportunities with that company.
When identifying occupations of interest, consider your personality — your distinctive qualities and traits. Do not rely on family history, parents' occupations, or friends' opinions.

One of the most effective methods of finding job leads is asking friends and acquaintances. They often know about openings, resignations, and retirements. Retirees, nonworkers, and minors are usually less connected to current job openings.

Like a product's packaging, your personal appearance is the 'brand' that helps you sell yourself to employers. An appropriate appearance demonstrates your value and quality. It does not have to be costly — it requires sensible choices in personal health, hygiene, and grooming.
Your daily grooming habits directly impact your personal hygiene. Good hygiene is essential for projecting a professional image.
When in doubt about what to wear to work, choose the conservative option. Work clothes should project a positive image and not distract customers or coworkers.

Volunteering is one of the best ways to gain work experience when you lack a professional track record. For example, volunteering at an animal shelter helps build credentials for animal-related careers.

Common substances many people underestimate: coffee contains caffeine and can be habit-forming, even though it is not widely considered harmful. More serious addictive substances include cigarettes, alcohol, and prescription painkillers.

A business alliance organization that operates at a local level. Example: the Seattle Metropolitan Chamber of Commerce. Members unite to influence policy, advocate for issues, and solve common local business problems. Contrast: the National Restaurant Association is national, and the International Federation of Social Workers is global.
Truly productive workers plan ahead so they can work at a relaxed pace. They accomplish more than those who fail to plan and rush at the last minute.

Any unwelcome sexual remarks, advances, conduct, or requests in the workplace. Employees have the right not to be sexually harassed. Different from age discrimination, cultural supremacy, or ethnocentrism.
Unemployment caused by periods of low economic demand due to the business cycle. Occurs when the economy slows, consumer spending falls, production decreases, and businesses lay off workers.

It is fine to share casual personal topics (TV shows, weekend activities). Avoid: divorce details (suggests distraction), career aspirations at other companies (signals disloyalty), and religious beliefs (can offend). These are off-limits because they may affect work relationships or perceptions.
Requires participants to recruit new members; revenue comes from recruitment fees rather than real products or services.
The ability to recognize how your actions affect others and understand your own emotions and biases. Foundation of emotional intelligence.
The ability to control your emotions, impulses, and behaviors. Helps eliminate negative thinking and maintain a positive attitude.
Understanding and sharing the feelings of others. Considering how your actions affect people.
Using your own emotional awareness and awareness of others to manage interactions successfully.
Focuses on the good side of things. People with positive attitudes handle setbacks better and are more productive.
Belief in your own abilities. Demonstrated through appropriate eye contact, firm handshake, and upright posture. Important because it helps you handle setbacks and communicate assertively.
The ability and willingness to think or act without being told.
Taking ownership of actions — both successes and failures. A key leadership trait.
When a person is inspired, s/he is driven to take action. Inspired individuals are engaged and motivated, not bored or disengaged.
A high achiever is persistent — doesn't give up when things are difficult. Often viewed as a leader because of strong work ethic.
Understanding another person's feelings and perspective.
Treating people equitably and without favoritism. Companies that fail to emphasize fairness and respect lose employees to turnover.

Working with others to accomplish shared goals. Effective collaboration among coworkers requires mutual respect — being willing to share ideas, appreciating coworkers' contributions, and acknowledging each person's right to express different opinions. Collaboration does NOT require similar personality traits, authoritarian attitudes, or selective communication.

The foundation of effective collaboration. Means valuing others' contributions, listening to different opinions, and treating everyone with dignity. Without mutual respect, collaborative efforts break down.
The customs, habits, and traditions of a particular group. People can belong to more than one culture at a time.
Reducing costs by 5% — easy to measure. Contrast with 'find more customers' which is vague.
Takes ownership of problems, ensures they are fixed, and takes steps to prevent recurrence.

A leadership technique primarily about listening — coaches must truly understand where the other person is coming from before offering guidance. First step in the coaching process: identify the problem.
Methods to boost morale. Low-cost examples: saying 'thank you,' giving verbal praise. These are effective and virtually free.
The number of employees reporting directly to one manager. Narrow span = few direct reports (like 2). Wide span = many direct reports.
A preconceived opinion not based on reason or experience. To overcome: acknowledge it, then monitor and challenge your biased thoughts.
The almost automatic response most people have to criticism is anger — a normal defensive reaction. It must be controlled through self-management.
Employers and supervisors offer criticism so workers can improve their job performance, not to attack them personally. The criticism should relate to your role as an employee.
Proper response: listen openly, make eye contact, and ask clarifying questions — not defensiveness.
A lack of interest in your own activities or the activities of others. Negative people are often indifferent. Contrast: a positive attitude shows interest in the job and in helping customers.
An alternate course of action prepared in case the primary plan does not work. Developing a contingency plan is an example of adaptability.
Your ability to empathize with someone is limited by your self-understanding — you must comprehend your own thoughts, attitudes, and actions before you can truly identify similar qualities in others.

A personal vision acts as a guidebook for your life, helping you make decisions at crossroads and new decision points. It is personal and cannot guide others' choices. Not useful for everyday decisions like grocery shopping.

It is important to learn how to handle conflicting viewpoints because you will face them many times in your personal and professional life. Other people's opinions are not always wrong, and it is possible for people to change their minds.

A text editor is a simplified type of word processor designed for users who need to write and edit text quickly with less focus on formatting. A word processor includes more formatting features for typical documents.
Can occur at any point in the change process. Dealing with resistance is an important leadership skill. Addressing resistance early is easier but not always possible.

If you fear change, the best way to cope is to face your fear head-on. Acknowledge the fear, then deal with the change one step at a time. Passive approaches — pretending the fear doesn't exist, hoping it goes away — do not help you overcome it.

People commonly resist change because they fear the unknown — they are unsure how change will affect their work or what the changes involve. A positive attitude and focus on the big picture (rather than immediate disruptions) helps people adapt.
To help people impacted by a change to the status quo, show understanding for their loss. Acknowledging what they are losing helps them accept the change.
When people just follow others without thinking, they are demonstrating herd mentality — the desire to fit in and do what everyone else is doing without critical thought.
Occurs when bad decisions are made by a group because members fear expressing opinions or ideas others might disagree with. The group prioritizes harmony over critical thinking.
A preference to maintain the status quo; the view that a change from the current situation represents a loss. Leads to resistance even when change would be beneficial.

A company's employees play a large role in achieving organizational goals. Involving and incentivizing employees in the achievement of organizational objectives leads to higher engagement and better outcomes. Without employee effort and commitment, organizational goals are unlikely to be met. Example: a pizzeria that assigns extra duties and offers pay bonuses for hitting a sales goal — and subsequently exceeds that goal every night — demonstrates the power of involving employees in achieving organizational objectives.
All the ways in which people differ from one another — includes both primary and secondary dimensions.
Inborn differences that cannot be changed. Examples: age, race/ethnicity, nationality, sexual orientation, certain physical and mental traits, and date/place of birth.
Characteristics that may change throughout one's lifetime. Examples: language, religion, income, geographic location, family status, work experience, communication style, and education level.

Understanding and respecting cultural differences. Best strategies: educate yourself about other cultures by asking respectfully, reading, or attending cultural events and diversity training. Do not joke about cultures, demand conformity in dress, or ignore cultural differences.

Employees should respect coworkers' privacy — for example, leaving the room when a coworker takes a private telephone call. Businesses should limit which employees can access customers' personal information to prevent unauthorized use.
Characterized by respect for both your own personal rights AND the rights of others. Stands up for oneself without violating others' rights. The ideal professional behavior style.
Characterized by a willingness to ignore the rights of others and exploit them to achieve personal goals. Focuses on winning at others' expense. NOT the same as assertive.
Characterized by a failure to exercise one's own personal rights and a lack of respect for personal needs. Gives in to others excessively. Not the same as assertive.
Behavior that is unpleasant or displeasing to others. Distinct from aggressive behavior.
The most important trust-building technique. If you don't do what you say you'll do, it is difficult for others to count on you. Example: covering a coworker's shift as promised.
Four key methods: (1) Keep your promises. (2) Communicate often and openly. (3) Do not share confidential information. (4) Take responsibility for your mistakes.
The ability to be comfortable with not knowing what will happen or what the correct action is. Allows you to accept the unknown without rushing to fill in blanks with wrong or incomplete information.

Allowing yourself to accept uncertainty rather than quickly looking up an answer or assuming you know. Demonstrates intellectual patience and openness. Example: Louis doesn't immediately look up unknown information on his phone.

The ability to convince others to adopt an idea, proposal, or course of action. A key interpersonal skill. Example: convincing your parents to give you and your siblings a weekly allowance is 'selling' the idea of an allowance.

Change leaders must be careful to avoid linear thinking — a rigid, step-by-step mode of thought that prevents creative problem-solving. Effective change leaders think flexibly, consider multiple possibilities, and avoid being locked into one path.

Proactively expressing yourself in social or professional situations — for example, starting a conversation with a stranger at a workshop. Demonstrates confidence and openness without being aggressive.

A group decision-making process that seeks agreement from all members. A disadvantage: it can lead to unnecessary compromise — watering down ideas or decisions to please everyone rather than choosing the best option.

The informal power dynamics and social behaviors in a workplace. Employees can successfully navigate office politics and prevent unnecessary drama by refusing to spread false rumors and maintaining professionalism.

Personal traits can change in response to feedback or consequences. Example: an employee repeatedly warned about arriving late who then starts arriving early is demonstrating a change in personal/behavioral traits.

Making assumptions about people based on their membership in a group rather than individual characteristics. Example: assuming all young people are not hard workers is stereotyping. Harmful because it leads to unfair treatment and poor decision-making.

A mindset in which a person believes they are controlled by external forces (other people, circumstances) rather than taking personal responsibility. Example: believing that everything bad happens because of others rather than acknowledging your own role. The opposite of proactivity.
Fulfilling one's obligations through words and actions in a dependable manner. Responsible people keep their commitments, making them dependable and trustworthy.
A 'no-compromise' negotiation approach in which a party insists on their terms and refuses to give ground. Often leads to win-lose outcomes.
When you accept responsibility for your own actions rather than blaming others, people are able to depend on you — a key element of trust and positive professional relationships.
People who foresee and prepare for change are in a better position to control their own future. Proactive adaptation is more effective than waiting for or avoiding change.
Creative people typically prefer to work under minimal supervision. They thrive with freedom to explore ideas rather than under close, authoritative oversight.

term: Ethical Dilemma — Check Your Gut Feeling
definition: One step in the ethical dilemma response process is checking your gut feeling — after gathering facts and considering all potential actions, you trust your intuition about what is right.

Intrinsic motivation comes from within (personal satisfaction, values, interest). Extrinsic motivation comes from external rewards (money, praise). In most developed countries, intrinsic motivation is generally more effective for sustained engagement.

To reduce bribery risk in selling situations, businesses should develop entertainment guidelines — clear policies about permissible gifts, meals, or entertainment when interacting with clients or vendors.
Building positive political relationships within an organization is beneficial because it helps employees and the company accomplish objectives through cooperation and resource access.
Achieving a healthy balance between work and personal life by budgeting your time and energy deliberately. Key: plan time allocation rather than always defaulting to work first.
One effective way to improve your rate of achieving goals is to tell others about them. Social accountability from sharing goals motivates follow-through.
Listening carefully to criticism — rather than becoming defensive — shows that you take it seriously and are open to growth. It is a key professional skill.
Using company property (car, equipment, time) for personal purposes during work hours is unethical — it constitutes misusing company resources, even for managers.

Spreading workplace gossip is often unethical because it communicates harmful information that can damage reputations, create conflict, and undermine trust. Gossip is illegal when it crosses into defamation.

Making decisions without seeking input from others — using minimum input. While sometimes appropriate for routine decisions, important decisions typically benefit from gathering input from multiple perspectives.

Assigning roles and responsibilities based on the natural strengths and preferences of different personality types in a group. Example: a natural leader takes the leadership role; others who work better with direction take supporting roles.

term: Looking at Problems from Various Viewpoints
definition: Examining a problem from multiple angles and perspectives leads to creative solutions and prevents narrow, one-dimensional thinking. This is a key benefit of diversity and collaborative problem-solving.

A basic requirement for effective decision-making is data — facts, information, and evidence that can inform the choice. Making decisions without data relies on guesswork rather than informed analysis.

A key step in problem-solving is evaluating whether your solution actually worked. After implementing a solution, check the outcome — if the customer or situation seems resolved, the solution was effective.

term: Treat All People with Respect = Empathy and Tact
definition: A practical technique for treating all types of people with respect — regardless of their background or behavior — is to use empathy (understanding others' feelings) and tact (communicating thoughtfully and diplomatically).

Effective feedback uses constructive, forward-looking language. Example: 'Maybe next time you could try this' is better than 'You shouldn't have done that.' Good feedback focuses on improvement, not blame.

term: Manage Work Commitments = Estimate Task Time
definition: To manage work commitments in a timely manner, businesspeople should estimate the time required for each task. Realistic time estimation prevents overcommitting and helps with prioritization and scheduling.

Positive and negative results in a business can often be directly related to the company's own actions — decisions, strategies, and behaviors. This reflects the principle of accountability in business.

Adapting products, services, or communication styles to conform to local customs, preferences, and cultural norms. Example: changing parts of a game for other countries to match local customs. This is distinct from globalization (standardizing across markets).

Involving employees in the decision-making process leading up to a planned change helps them become receptive to the change rather than resistant. People resist change less when they feel heard and included.

When confronting another person about a conflict, it is important to avoid 'you' statements (which sound accusatory and put people on the defensive). Instead, use 'I' statements that describe your feelings and perspective.
If you always act ethically in all situations — regardless of who is watching or what the context is — you are demonstrating being consistent. Ethical consistency is a key component of integrity.

Even when eager to start a new project, it is important to plan before taking action. Taking time to plan prevents mistakes, ensures resources are allocated correctly, and increases the likelihood of success.

Knowing your personal strengths and weaknesses helps you make informed decisions about your future — choosing the right career path, knowing when to ask for help, and setting realistic goals. It is not primarily for testing or identifying others' abilities.

term: Aggressive People = Hard to Feel Needs Matter
definition: Being around aggressive people can make it difficult for you to believe that your needs are as important as the needs of others. Aggressive behavior dominates interactions and can undermine your sense of self-worth in the moment.

When employees' personal interests become more important than the company's overall goals, informal political relationships form — which often cause organizational conflict. These relationships can fragment teamwork and create rivalries.

A personal diary is a useful self-reflection tool for identifying patterns in your own behavior — noticing recurring reactions, emotions, or situations that affect your performance. The value is in pattern recognition, not just recording daily events.

term: Low Self-Management = Threw File, Slammed Door
definition: When someone throws a file, slams a door, or storms out of a meeting in frustration, they are demonstrating low self-management skills — the inability to regulate emotional reactions in professional settings.

term: Adapt to New Situation = Make a Choice
definition: When adapting to a new or challenging situation, you must always make a choice — decide how you will respond. Adaptation requires intentional decision-making rather than passive reaction. You can't always control circumstances, but you can always choose your response.

term: Sharing a Vision = Stay Flexible Under Pressure
definition: A guideline for effectively sharing a vision is being flexible — keeping the team focused even when outsiders respond negatively or circumstances change. Staying flexible allows the vision to evolve while maintaining its core direction.

Mental ability is a dimension of individual differences that involves cognitive capacity in specific areas. Example: Patrick is great at math but struggles with Spanish; Cat excels in Spanish but struggles with math — they have different mental abilities, not different personalities or lifestyles.

When preparing to do business in another culture, it is important to research that culture's customs, etiquette, and communication norms first. Example: before traveling to Scotland for a business deal, researching Scottish cultural practices avoids offensive misunderstandings.

term: High Self-Concept = Believe You Are Valuable
definition: When your self-concept is high, you believe that you are a valuable person — capable, worthy of respect, and able to contribute. High self-concept supports assertive behavior and resilience. Low self-concept leads to self-doubt and passive behavior.

People who receive a great deal of negative feedback may develop depression or emotional distress. This is why constructive, balanced feedback is important — relentless negative feedback can damage self-esteem and wellbeing.

Employees who set realistic goals and standards for themselves are often able to control their level of stress. Unrealistic goals create anxiety and frustration; achievable goals build confidence and reduce pressure.

term: Sense of Humor = Eases Team Tension
definition: Maintaining a sense of humor helps you be a better team member because it eases tension when disagreements or stress arise. It keeps the atmosphere positive and prevents minor conflicts from escalating — while also boosting morale.

term: Goal = Concrete and Measurable; Vision = Abstract
definition: A key distinction: a goal is concrete and measurable (specific, actionable, achievable by a deadline). A vision is abstract and indefinite — a broad future aspiration. Goals are the steps taken to achieve the vision.

One way to assess your personal strengths and weaknesses is to identify your interests — activities you enjoy and feel drawn to often align with natural strengths. Understanding interests helps guide skill development and career direction.

term: Cannot Take Blame = Cannot Take Credit
definition: If you cannot take the blame for your mistakes (denying responsibility), then you also cannot take the credit for your successes. Accountability works both ways — people who only want credit but deflect blame are seen as dishonest.

It is unethical to spread malicious gossip about a coworker because the gossip may damage the person's reputation. False or harmful rumors can harm someone's career, relationships, and emotional wellbeing.

One effective way to persuade others is to establish personal credibility — demonstrating that you are knowledgeable, experienced, and trustworthy regarding the topic. People follow those they believe have relevant expertise.

When feeling overwhelmed by a stressful job, a healthy coping strategy is to talk through the situation with a trusted friend or coworker. Verbalizing stress reduces its emotional weight and can provide perspective and practical advice.

term: Assessing Long-Term Impact = More Ethical Action
definition: One benefit of assessing the long-term value and impact of your actions on others is that it helps you act more ethically. When you consider consequences beyond the immediate moment, you make more responsible and principled decisions.

term: Following Rules = Rule of Law Principle
definition: Following your teacher's or supervisor's guidelines — even when you disagree — is an example of the rule of law principle: respecting established rules and authority structures. This is a foundational ethical principle of organized society.

term: First Step Ethical Dilemma = Identify It
definition: The first step in resolving an ethical dilemma is to identify the dilemma — recognizing that an ethical issue exists in the situation. You cannot address a problem you haven't named. Subsequent steps: figure out who's affected, consider potential actions, reflect on outcomes.

Destructive criticism attacks the person rather than the behavior and offers no constructive path forward. Example: 'You must be stupid if you don't know how to do that by now.' Contrast with constructive criticism: 'I would prefer you do it this way.'

term: Too Much Initiative = Hard Feelings Among Employees
definition: While taking initiative is generally positive, taking too much initiative can cause hard feelings among employees — if it makes them feel bypassed, undervalued, or overshadowed. The key is balancing proactivity with collaboration.

Over-committing to too many assignments — without realistic time management — leads to lower quality of work. When deadlines pile up, individuals rush, miss details, and produce subpar outputs. Quality suffers when quantity of commitments exceeds capacity.

Valuing diversity means acknowledging differences (in background, perspective, skills, culture) as assets — not problems, not ignored, not minimized. Diverse teams generate more creative solutions and serve broader markets more effectively.

When your level of self-esteem is low, this causes you to have doubts about yourself — questioning your abilities, worth, and potential. Low self-esteem can lead to hesitation, risk-avoidance, and underperformance.

term: Happy Proud Memory = Determine Your Values
definition: Thinking of a time when you were happy and proud can help you determine what values made you feel that way. Tracing positive emotions back to their source reveals core values — principles that matter most to you.

term: Ethical Dilemmas = Treat Them as Challenges
definition: Ethical dilemmas should be thought of as challenges — opportunities to practice integrity and demonstrate character. Viewing them as challenges (rather than threats or emergencies) promotes thoughtful, principled responses.

The use of video cameras to monitor employees in the workplace is considered a possible violation of employees' privacy — particularly in personal spaces (bathrooms, break rooms). Employers must balance security needs with employee privacy rights.

When a person continually resists change or fails to adapt to new circumstances, their level of stress often increases. Resistance creates tension between reality (which changes) and the person's desire for the familiar. Adaptability reduces stress.

term: First Step Leading Change = Show Why
definition: The first step in the process of leading change is to show why the change is necessary — explaining the problem or opportunity that requires a new approach. Without a compelling 'why,' people resist rather than embrace change.

Being enthusiastic about your job is an example of having a good attitude — a behavior that builds positive working relationships. Enthusiasm is contagious; it motivates teammates and signals commitment to the work.

term: Preparing to Sell an Idea = Determine Details
definition: Before attempting to persuade others to accept an idea, you should prepare by determining the details of your idea — understanding exactly what it involves, what resources it requires, and how it works. This preparation builds your credibility.

Managers are more likely to be able to persuade employees to follow new procedures if they have credibility — meaning employees trust the manager's knowledge and judgment. Without credibility, directives meet resistance.

term: Achieve Vision = Hard Work and Dedication
definition: After writing or sharing a personal vision, you must commit to achieving it through hard work and dedication. Vision without action remains a wish; consistent effort over time transforms vision into reality.

term: SMART Goal = Put Goals in Writing
definition: One of the tips for smart goal setting is putting your goals in writing. Written goals are more concrete, memorable, and trackable. Research consistently shows that written goals are achieved at higher rates than unwritten ones.

term: Raise Self-Esteem = Talk to Someone You Trust
definition: One good way to raise your level of self-esteem when it has dropped is to discuss your feelings with someone you trust. Trusted feedback, encouragement, and perspective from others can help rebuild confidence and a positive self-view.

term: Honesty and Integrity = Fair Effort for Fair Pay
definition: Workers can demonstrate their honesty and integrity by giving a fair amount of effort in exchange for their pay. Doing an honest day's work — not cutting corners or under-delivering — is the most fundamental expression of workplace integrity.

An effective persuasion technique is promoting benefits — explaining what the other person will gain by agreeing. Example: 'If we adopt this plan, the company's profitability will increase substantially.' Benefit-focused persuasion aligns the proposal with the audience's interests.

term: Business Vision = General so Everyone Can Relate
definition: An excellent business vision is general (not overly specific) because everyone in the organization must be able to relate to it. A vision that is too narrow excludes employees from different roles. A broad vision creates shared purpose across all levels.

term: Build Strategic Relationship = Find Shared Vision
definition: One technique for building a strategic relationship with an influential person is to find a shared vision — a common goal or belief. When two people align around a shared purpose, they have a natural basis for partnership and cooperation.

term: Employee Behaves Unethically = Follows the Crowd
definition: One of the main reasons employees behave unethically is to do what everyone else does — social conformity pressure. When unethical behavior is normalized in a workplace, individuals feel less accountable for joining in, even if they know it is wrong.
Requires two elements: sharing (a sender and receiver) and understanding (both grasp the same message).
Through established channels within a company's structure. Example: staff meeting announcement about bonuses. Usually involves serious topics.
Unofficial channels; casual conversation. Less reliable than formal channels.
Management sends messages to lower-level employees. Example: supervisor emails staff about a required meeting.
Employees send messages to supervisors. Example: an employee request to a manager.
Communication between people at the same level in the organization.
Provides information of interest to a specific group, often sent via email. Keeps customers or subscribers engaged.
A formal document presenting information on a topic. Long reports use headings and subheadings to organize different topics.

A type of written communication whose main purpose is to explain new policies, inform readers of changes, or provide facts in a way the reader can understand. Example: a business changing its credit policy writes an informational message to explain the change and how it benefits customers. NOT used for obtaining feedback or analyzing sales proposals.

A message designed to convince the reader to take a specific action. A collection letter — written to encourage delinquent customers to pay — is a type of persuasive message. Collection letters should be firm but never threatening or overly aggressive.

A legally binding promise not to disclose information about another party. Signed before finalizing a purchase or partnership to protect each party's private information. Different from a kickback (an illegal payment), an exclusive agreement (forbids buying from competitors), or a labor contract (union-company agreement on wages and conditions).
Paying full attention to the speaker. Demonstrated through nodding, smiling, maintaining eye contact, and not interrupting.
Restating what the speaker said in your own words. Active listeners paraphrase to confirm the speaker's message and demonstrate understanding.

An effective way to support and encourage someone talking to you: make short comments such as 'yes' or 'I see' occasionally. This signals you are listening and hearing what the speaker says. Do not interrupt to debate, and do not stay completely silent — occasional short comments show active engagement.

A barrier to listening that comes from within the listener — personal thoughts, worries, or preoccupations that prevent full attention. Example: being unable to focus on a friend because you are worried about a family member's job loss. Distinct from external/environmental distractions (noise, poor lighting).
The ability to use words effectively. An important skill for careers in most fields.
