Matching your communication style — tone, pace, vocabulary, body language — to that of your audience. One of the most effective ways to build rapport. Customers generally feel more comfortable when the other person communicates in ways familiar to them. Mirroring is a legitimate communication strategy, not dishonest or deceptive.

Establishing a sense of connection and trust with another person. Achieved through mirroring, active listening, showing genuine interest, and communicating in a way that feels familiar and comfortable to the other party.
Maintaining open and honest communication within an organization. When companies are transparent about goals and strategies, employees understand their roles better.
Formal guidelines that keep employees and customers safe from internal and external harm.
Defiance of authority — speaking disrespectfully to a supervisor or refusing commands. A serious workplace violation.

Oral directions are spoken instructions (e.g., a parent telling you to vacuum). Written directions appear in text form (e.g., a manual or memo). Nonverbal directions use gestures or signals rather than words. These are distinct — spoken directions are oral, not written or nonverbal.

At the beginning of a research report, the writer should include the purpose of the study — what the research aims to find out and why. Appendices (supporting materials), bibliography (sources), and the solution to the problem all appear at the END of the report, not the beginning.

Open questions cannot be answered with a simple yes/no and invite a full response. Example: 'What is your presentation about?' is an open question. Closed questions restrict the answer: 'Is your presentation finished?' (yes/no). Open questions are more effective for gathering detailed information.

A type of visual aid used in business documents or presentations to explain processes and relationships. Best for showing how steps connect or flow from one to another. Different from bar graphs (compare quantities), line graphs (show trends over time), and pie charts (show proportions of a whole).

A written report that provides a permanent record of simple but important daily facts and events. Often used to document what work was completed, what was planned, and any issues that arose during a working day.

A communication technology that enables multiple people in different locations to participate in a meeting simultaneously, with both audio and video. More efficient than voice memos or emails when real-time discussion is needed across locations.

When giving an oral presentation to a large group, speakers should speak loudly enough for the audience to hear the message clearly. Other guidelines: avoid swaying back and forth (distracting nonverbal), maintain eye contact, and use visual aids when sharing relevant information.

A business letter written to confirm the details of an order, meeting, or agreement. Must contain all the necessary information so both parties are aligned on terms, dates, quantities, and other key details.

Before beginning to read a text, an effective reading strategy is to think about what you want to learn from it. This focuses attention and improves comprehension. Other strategies include consulting a glossary or taking notes, but thinking about learning goals comes first.

When responding to an official request for proposal (RFP), the writer should follow the directions specified in the request document. The proposal should provide formal objectives, respond to the specific requirements outlined, and be structured as the client requests.

Casual, inattentive listening with low engagement — the listener hears sounds but is not actively processing meaning. Example: casually listening to a podcast while doing something else. Contrast with active listening (focused, engaged, seeking understanding).

A correct interpretation of a nonverbal cue: fidgeting typically signals nervousness or impatience. Other common nonverbal cues: crossed arms = closed off/defensive, clenched fists = tension, steady eye contact = engagement/confidence.

Organizing a speech or presentation by time order — moving from earliest to latest events. Example: a speech about Abraham Lincoln that covers his early political career, presidency, then death. Common organizational patterns also include topical, spatial, and cause-and-effect.

The section of a formal report containing supplemental information that supports the main content but is not essential to the body of the report. When a writer has relevant information that would interrupt the flow of the report, it goes in the appendix.

A key best practice for professional email: always proofread the email before sending it. Other email guidelines: include a descriptive subject line, respond promptly, avoid all caps, and don't use texting abbreviations.

To follow written directions effectively, complete the steps in the order they are written. Do not skip ahead or reorder steps. Ideally, read all directions first to understand the full process before beginning.

Individuals who can defend their ideas objectively usually provide others with logical evidence — facts, data, and reasoning — rather than personal opinions, emotional appeals, or unrelated statistics.

An executive summary sequences its main ideas in the same order that they appear in the full report — making it a condensed parallel version of the complete document. It should be approximately one-fourth to one-third as long as the full report.

When the majority of readers are likely to support a report's content, the writer should format it for a receptive audience. The format and tone can be more direct and less defensive than reports written for skeptical audiences.

A type of simple report used by businesspeople to periodically inform others about the status of ongoing activities. Example: a buyer prepares a progress report to inform other departments about the status of scheduled deliveries.

Writing created by two or more people working together. It is especially important to allow plenty of time for revision so multiple contributors can review, refine, and align on the final document before submission.

A short, informal business report used for everyday communication. Examples: weekly status reports, progress reports, and daily activity reports. Contrast with formal reports (longer, structured, submitted for major decisions).

A document that analyzes the possible implications, viability, or outcomes of a proposed plan, product, or change. Completed before undertaking a project or initiative. Example: explaining the possible implications of implementing a cooperative advertising program.

When a message contains both good and bad news, effective structure places the good news at the beginning and the bad news in a secondary position (not at the start). This approach softens the impact and maintains the reader's goodwill.

A good telephone voice varies tone to reflect interest and enthusiasm — do not speak in a monotone. Also: project sincerity, be easy to understand, and maintain a friendly tone. Avoid decisiveness or stern control, which give a negative impression.

When answering, give both the name of the business AND your own name. Customers feel more comfortable dealing with individuals who have identified themselves. Not necessary to give the address, company purpose, or time of day unless asked.
Immediately thank the caller. In many cases the caller is a customer leaving an important message — thanking them is a professional courtesy.

Requires shutting out distractions, setting aside what you are working on, and focusing on what the speaker is saying. Good telephone listening is a skill learned through practice, not natural ability. There will still be times when you need to ask questions.

Your attitude is projected over the phone — a smile can actually be heard in your voice. Smiling when you answer the phone indicates a positive attitude and puts the caller at ease. A frown signals a negative attitude. Technique: smile when you answer the phone.

To answer interview questions adequately, an applicant must listen carefully to the interviewer to fully understand what is being asked. Talking rapidly or responding quickly without listening first leads to poor answers. Dressing appropriately helps make a good impression, but listening carefully is what allows you to answer questions well.

When a supervisor must address a sensitive, personal, or confidential issue (such as salary) with an employee, the appropriate method is a private face-to-face meeting. Company newsletters, telephone conference calls, and memos are for communicating with multiple people at once, not for private individual matters.

A leading question suggests the desired answer within the question itself. Example: 'Mia is a great manager, isn't she?' steers the respondent toward agreement. Leading questions are also a survey design flaw — example: 'Do you agree that the government wastes taxpayers' money by supporting unnecessary programs?' Contrast with open-ended or neutral questions.

A memorandum (memo) is a simple written report typically distributed to employees within a business. It is an internal communication tool used for routine updates, policy changes, or reminders. Memos are less formal than business letters and are intended for an internal audience.

Internal reports convey information within an organization. A common example is a report containing new personnel regulations — distributed to employees to explain updated HR policies, benefits changes, or compliance requirements.

The very first step before organizing and delivering an oral presentation is to determine the purpose or nature of the message. Knowing why you are presenting guides what information to include, how to organize it, and how to tailor it to the audience.

Graphic aids in presentations are visual elements such as charts, diagrams, graphs, or pictures that enhance understanding of the content. They are not references to clothing style, facial expressions, or interpersonal dialogue.

In a problem/solution organizational format, the writer or speaker first states the problem or dilemma at hand, then explains the proposed answer or solution. This is a logical structure for persuasive papers and presentations.

Nonverbal communication includes body language that conveys messages without words. When someone extends a hand for a handshake and the other person ignores it and keeps hands in pockets, the nonverbal message is: 'I don't want to know you' — a sign of disinterest or rejection.

A credibility appeal (also called an ethos appeal) persuades the audience by citing the authority or expertise of the source. Example: 'This book is written by Dr. Juliet Browne, who holds a doctorate from Harvard and chairs the World Health Organization.' The credentials establish trust.

Simple memorandums written by business employees are typically intended for an internal audience — colleagues, managers, or staff within the same organization. They are not meant for suppliers, boards of directors, or external planning committees.

term: Group Problem-Solving Disadvantage = Takes More Time
definition: A primary disadvantage of group problem-solving is that it takes more time than individual decision-making. Coordinating schedules, reaching consensus, and managing group dynamics all add to the time required to arrive at a solution.

The best way to support a speaker and show interest during a presentation is to take notes of the speaker's most important comments. This demonstrates active engagement, helps you retain information, and shows respect to the presenter.

A characteristic of effective oral presentations is that they attract attention — through a compelling opening, strong delivery, and relevant content. Other key traits: clear organization, appropriate visual aids, and audience engagement.

A geographical map is the type of visual aid used to clearly illustrate data that varies by location — such as a company's sales broken down by region. Venn diagrams show relationships; org charts show hierarchy; flowcharts show processes.

Persuasive language uses words and phrases designed to motivate action. Example: 'Yes, you'll save money instantly' uses direct, benefit-focused language. Persuasive words address the audience's interests — contrast with threatening language, impersonal messages, or neutral descriptions.

term: Inductive Organization = Benefits First, Conclusion Last
definition: Inductive organization (also called indirect or indirect order) presents supporting evidence, reasons, or benefits first, then draws the conclusion at the end. Useful when the audience may be resistant. Contrast: deductive = conclusion first, then support.

term: Memorandum Heading = To, From, Date, Subject
definition: The standard heading of a memorandum includes four elements: To (recipient), From (sender), Date, and Subject. It does not typically include a salutation, company name at the top, or title line.

The primary purpose of staff meetings is to share information — updates, announcements, and coordination among team members. They are not typically used to conduct research, achieve a task, or develop a specific message.

Technical jargon — specialized industry terminology — is appropriate when communicating with coworkers who share the same knowledge base. It is NOT appropriate with new customers, general acquaintances, or in introductory conversations where the listener may not understand the terms.

term: Posting Job Info on Personal Social Media = Inappropriate
definition: It is considered inappropriate and unprofessional for an employee to post company job opening information on their personal social media accounts. This can give personal connections an unfair advantage over other candidates and uses the company's brand for personal purposes.

term: Defending Idea Objectively = Citing Scientific Reports
definition: To defend an idea objectively during a persuasive presentation or argument, the most credible approach is citing scientific reports, data, or research — not personal opinions or anecdotal evidence. Objective defense relies on factual, verifiable information.

An effective way to conclude a persuasive presentation is to challenge the audience to take action. This reinforces the persuasive purpose, creates urgency, and motivates the audience to act on the information they've received.

term: Formal Downward Communication = HR Memo to All
definition: Formal downward communication flows from management to employees through official channels. A common example is an HR memo sent to all employees about policy or benefit changes. It is formal (official channel), downward (from authority to staff), and mass (all employees).

term: Write Proposals = Research the Problem First
definition: Before writing a proposal, employees must research the problem — understanding the issue, gathering data, and identifying possible solutions. Writing the proposal without prior research would result in vague goals rather than specific, evidence-based recommendations.

The three main purposes of oral presentations are: (1) To inform — share knowledge or facts, (2) To persuade — convince the audience to accept an idea or take action, (3) To entertain — engage and interest the audience. All other purposes (like instructing or debating) fall under one of these three.

term: Project Status Meeting = Be Prepared to Update
definition: When participating in a project status meeting, it is critical to be prepared to provide an update on your portion of the project. Participants should know their current progress, any obstacles, and upcoming milestones — not monopolize conversation or discuss unrelated topics.

An audience shows support for a speaker's opinion or effort by applauding. Applause is a positive nonverbal response that reinforces the speaker's message and shows appreciation. Heckling or ignoring the speaker are the opposite.

When taking a telephone message for a coworker, you should ask the caller for their name, organization, and telephone number. This gives the coworker the essential information needed to return the call and identify the context of the message.

term: Organize Pricing Deductively = Least to Most Expensive
definition: When organizing pricing packages deductively for presentation, the information is listed from the least expensive to the most expensive option. This is a deductive (direct) approach — presenting straightforward facts in a logical progression.

A key guideline for following written instructions correctly is not to rush. Rushing increases the likelihood of skipping steps or misreading instructions — leading to errors. Other guidelines: know the end result, go in order, and accept responsibility.

Persuasive writing is designed to convince the reader to take action or adopt a viewpoint. An online banner advertisement is an example of persuasive writing — its purpose is to persuade viewers to click or make a purchase. Contrast: informative writing (facts only), responsive writing (replies to input).

When delivering difficult or sensitive news — such as reducing an employee's hours due to budget cuts — the best channel is an in-person meeting. In-person communication allows for emotional acknowledgment, questions, and a more humane experience than email, letter, or phone.

To prepare effectively for a meeting, the first step is to review the meeting agenda. The agenda outlines the topics to be discussed, allowing participants to gather relevant information, prepare contributions, and arrive ready to engage meaningfully.

term: Effective Oral Communication = Pronounce Words Correctly
definition: A key characteristic of effective oral communication is pronouncing words correctly. Clear enunciation ensures the audience understands the message. Other essentials: appropriate pace, confident tone, and organized content. Impressing listeners or distracting attention are not goals.

term: Writing Clear Instructions = Understand the Process
definition: A guideline for writing clear instructions for coworkers is to understand the process you are explaining before writing it out. You cannot write clear, accurate steps if you do not fully understand the sequence and purpose of each action.

term: Opening Business Letter = Go Right to the Point
definition: The opening of an effective business letter should go right to the point — stating the purpose immediately. Business letters are professional communications, and readers appreciate directness. Avoid lengthy preamble, impersonal openings, or excessive background before reaching the main message.

term: Cause and Effect Report = Actions and Results
definition: A cause-and-effect report describes relationships between actions and their probable results. Example: Liam wrote a report describing relationships between actions and their probable results — this is a cause-and-effect research report. Different from analytical, interpretive, or argumentative reports.
Individuals and businesses own the means of production and compete freely. Characterized by private ownership, freedom of choice, competition, and profit motive.
The value of the next-best alternative you give up when making a choice.
The gap between unlimited wants for goods and services and the limited resources available. Scarcity forces people and businesses to make choices.
As price increases, quantity demanded decreases (and vice versa).
High demand, low supply, rising prices. Best time to be a seller.
High supply, low demand, falling prices. Best time to be a buyer.
Demand that changes significantly when price changes. Common for luxury goods and non-necessities. Elastic demand goods are more price-sensitive.
Demand that stays fairly constant even if price changes. Common for necessities. Example: gasoline — people buy close to the same amount regardless of price changes.
The amount of money a consumer has available to spend. If consumers don't have money, they cannot buy products — regardless of price.
Tangible items that last a long time. Examples: cars, cell phones, laptops, appliances.
Quickly consumed or wear out quickly. Examples: fruit smoothies, gasoline, cosmetics, food.
Intangible activities. Examples: haircuts, banking, consulting.

Industrial goods that are constantly purchased and used up in the operation of a business but do NOT become part of the finished product. Example: cash register tape, cleaning supplies, printer paper.
Industrial goods that become part of the finished product after they have been processed. Example: steel used in car manufacturing, flour used in bread.
Industrial goods used in the operation of a business but not used in the actual production of a good or service. Examples: computers, forklifts, office furniture.
GDP rising, low unemployment, high consumer spending, rising interest rates and bond yields.
Highest point before a downturn.
GDP falling, rising unemployment, lower consumer spending and production. During contraction: consumer spending decreases most notably.
Lowest point; recovery begins from here.
The steady increase in the price of consumer goods and services over time. Salaries must also rise so consumers can afford higher prices.
A decrease in the general price level; the opposite of inflation.
Come into play when both wages and prices rise or fall at the same rate, so consumers are willing to pay the new price.

The ratio between two prices changes ONLY when the prices change by different amounts or percentages. If both prices double, halve, or change by the same proportion, the ratio stays the same. Example: red apples 60¢, green apples 80¢ — if red rises to 90¢ but green stays at 80¢, the ratio has changed.
Exports > imports. Increases GDP.
Imports > exports. Decreases GDP.
The total monetary value of all goods and services produced within a country in a given time period; the main measure of economic health.
A tax on imported goods.
The rapid and unimpeded flow of capital, labor, and ideas across national borders. One major impact: increased competition for businesses.

International agreements that eliminate or reduce trade barriers between countries, giving businesses more opportunities to buy and sell at the international level. However, for developing economies, free trade can sometimes cause job loss as cheaper foreign labor or goods displace local workers.

A cost advantage that occurs when a company produces at a larger scale than its competitors, resulting in a lower per-unit cost of production. Example: a large manufacturing plant operating at a lower per-unit cost than smaller competitors is benefiting from economies of scale.

Natural resources (land, water, minerals) are one of the factors of production. When natural resources are used in the process of producing goods or services, they are transformed and become capital resources.

A strike occurs when all or most employees stop working and leave their workplace to pressure an employer to meet their demands. This is the most direct form of employee pressure in labor disputes. Different from a boycott (consumers refusing to buy), arbitration (neutral third party decides), or mediation (neutral helper facilitates agreement).

An economic principle stating that as you add more of one input (like workers) while keeping other factors the same, at some point each additional unit of that input produces less additional output than the previous one. Example: hiring more workers increases output initially, but eventually adding even more workers causes output to decrease because they get in each other's way.

A market structure in which there are many competing businesses selling identical products, and no single seller can influence the market price. Pure competition has the most competition of any market structure. Contrast: monopoly (one seller), oligopoly (few sellers), monopolistic competition (many sellers, differentiated products).

term: Government Consumer Protection via Product Testing
definition: Governments protect consumers by establishing requirements for product testing. Before products can be sold to the public, many must pass safety and quality tests. This is a key way government balances the economy and protects consumer welfare.

A market structure in which the government permits one company to be the sole provider of a service because it would be impractical or wasteful to have multiple competitors. Example: in many areas, trash pickup is legally controlled by just one company — a regulated monopoly. Different from an illegal monopoly (achieved through anticompetitive behavior).

When supply exceeds demand, prices fall or sellers offer incentives to stimulate purchases. Example: energy companies offer free electricity at night and on weekends because during those times supply (production) is greater than demand (consumption).

When a business's operations are efficient and economical, it can produce quality products and offer them at competitive prices that consumers can afford. Efficiency benefits consumers directly through lower prices and better value.

Businesses that sell different types of products or services but compete for the same consumer spending. Example: Starbucks and American Eagle Outfitters are indirect competitors — they don't sell the same things but compete for the same discretionary spending. Different from direct competitors (who sell similar products to the same market).

Financial assistance provided by the government to businesses or industries. One way government supports business growth — subsidies reduce costs and encourage production or development in targeted sectors.

A monetary policy where a country fixes (pegs) its currency's exchange rate to the value of another currency, such as the U.S. dollar. Example: a developing country sets its currency equal to the USD. Different from a free-floating currency (whose value fluctuates based on market forces).

A desire for something that has monetary value — something that can be bought or sold. Example: wanting to see a movie at a theater is an economic want because it requires spending money. Contrast with noneconomic wants (desires that don't involve money, like friendship).

An individualist society has loose group ties — people focus on individual goals and self-reliance. A collectivist society has tight group ties — people prioritize group harmony. These cultural dimensions affect business behavior globally.

The items used to produce goods and services. Includes land (natural resources), labor (human effort), capital (equipment, tools), and entrepreneurship. Different from noneconomic resources (things not used in production).

term: Lifestyle and Fashion Trends Change Frequently
definition: A social factor that affects businesses: lifestyle and fashion trends change quite frequently, influencing what products consumers want. Businesses must monitor these trends to stay relevant to their target markets.

term: Consumer Spending Increases When Unemployment Declines
definition: Consumer spending is most likely to increase when unemployment rates decline — because more people have jobs and income to spend. Spending decreases when unemployment rises, interest rates spike, or recession begins.

One of the key benefits of competition to consumers is that it encourages businesses to develop new and better products and services. Competition drives innovation, quality improvement, and lower prices.

One of government's original roles in business was acting as a referee — ensuring fair competition, enforcing contracts, and maintaining marketplace order. Over time this role expanded to include consumer protection, regulation, and more.
As a country undergoes significant economic development and growth, it will most likely participate more in world trade — both importing and exporting — as its economy integrates with global markets.

An economic system where the government imposes high taxes to fund a wide variety of human services programs (healthcare, education, social safety nets). Different from a communist command economy (where government owns all means of production).

Production is a necessary precondition for consumption — goods and services must first be produced before they can be consumed. Economic activity order: production → exchange/distribution → consumption.

When demand for a good or service exceeds its available supply, the price typically rises. Sellers can charge more because buyers compete for scarce goods. Opposite: when supply exceeds demand, prices fall.
Increased export trade typically results in an increase in employment opportunities — as businesses need more workers to produce goods for foreign markets.
The process of issuing a government permit that allows an individual or business to operate legally. A way government regulates business activity.
The amount of goods and services produced by a business from a set amount of resources. Calculated by dividing output by input.

Better-trained and educated workers tend to be more productive because they are more organized, better able to manage their time wisely, more willing to adapt, and more creative. This is the reason — not simply that they earn more or become managers.

Business managers review financial information regularly to identify patterns and trends — such as which products are growing in popularity, which are declining, and which are most profitable. Financial data reveals sales trends, not operational details like delivery schedules.
Economic goods must be produced before they can be consumed.
Businesses that produce products are socially responsible to the users of those products — they should offer safe, quality goods.

Businesses increase profit by increasing revenue or decreasing expenses. One way to decrease expenses in the customer service area: eliminate free services (such as delivery or gift wrapping) or charge a fee for them. Adding free services or improving service quality INCREASES expenses, not profit.

Employees help a company increase sales by actively calling on customers to determine if they need additional products. This directly generates new sales. Other employee actions — advising customers of price increases, ordering inventory at a discount, or raising markups — do not directly increase sales volume.

Levels of inflation and unemployment are factors that affect competition in the world market. High inflation reduces purchasing power; high unemployment reduces consumer spending — both can make a nation's businesses less competitive globally.

term: Currency Exchange Rate Rises = High Consumer Confidence
definition: A nation's currency exchange rate is likely to increase when consumer confidence is high — because confident consumers spend more, boosting economic activity and attracting foreign investment. Contractions, shrinking labor force, or fluctuating stocks tend to lower exchange rates.

term: Private Property = Businesses Own What They Produce
definition: One of the characteristics of a free enterprise/market economy is the right to private property — businesses own the goods and services they produce. Contrast: in command economies, the government may own the means of production and output.

The two main sources of revenue used to pay the costs of government regulation and operations are taxes (collected from individuals and businesses) and borrowing (issuing government bonds or loans). Other government funding methods (like printing money) are less standard.

term: Limited Natural Resource = Replace Oil with Gas
definition: An example of finding another source for a limited natural resource is replacing oil with natural gas. As oil becomes scarcer or more expensive, switching to an alternative energy source addresses the resource limitation while continuing production.

term: Government Protects Property Through Zoning Laws
definition: The government protects business owners' property through the use of zoning laws — which regulate how land may be used in specific areas, protecting investments and ensuring orderly land development. Contrast: minimum wage laws protect workers; consumer protection laws protect buyers.

When certain commodities (raw materials or goods) are in limited supply, this drives up production costs. The resulting inflation — caused by rising input costs — is called cost-push inflation. Contrast: demand-pull inflation (caused by excessive consumer demand).

When demand for a product increases, a producer normally increases the quantity of product supplied — and the product's price will increase as well. Greater demand allows sellers to charge more. Opposite: declining demand leads to lower prices.

Government agencies protect the rights of businesses — ensuring fair competition, enforcing contracts, and protecting property rights. While governments also regulate businesses, they serve a dual role as protector of legitimate business interests.

term: Government Mandates Unleaded Gas = Protect Environment
definition: Government mandating the use of unleaded gas in new cars is an example of protecting the environment — reducing harmful emissions. This is a regulatory intervention aimed at environmental protection, not protecting specific businesses or stabilizing the economy.

term: Service Business = No Transfer of Ownership
definition: Service businesses use goods in order to perform their services but usually do not transfer ownership of those goods to customers. Example: a dry cleaner uses chemicals and equipment but doesn't give these to the customer — only the clean garment (service) is delivered.

term: Offer Products Internationally = Expand to New Markets
definition: A primary reason businesses decide to offer products in other countries is to expand to new, untapped markets — accessing consumers who haven't been reached yet. This strategy increases revenue potential and diversifies customer base.

term: Favorable Balance of Trade = Exports > Imports
definition: A nation has a favorable balance of trade (trade surplus) when its exports exceed its imports. Example: exports of $3.5 million and imports of $3.1 million = favorable. Contrast: an unfavorable balance (trade deficit) occurs when imports exceed exports.

term: Specialty Business = One or Few Product Lines
definition: A specialty business carries a considerable assortment of goods but only one or a few related product lines. Example: a shoe store carries many shoe styles but only sells shoes (and accessories). Contrast: a department store (many product lines) or a discount store (broad, price-focused selection).

An economic depression is characterized by high unemployment, severely reduced consumer spending, sharp decline in GDP, and widespread business failures. Contrast with a recession (milder downturn) or expansion (growth period).

term: Wide Fluctuating Interest Rates = Economic Instability
definition: Interest rates that rise and fall by a wide range on a regular basis are an indication of economic instability. Stable economies maintain relatively steady interest rates; volatility signals uncertainty for businesses and investors.

When grocery store baggers help customers carry groceries out to their cars, they are providing task utility — value added by performing a service (a physical task) that the customer would otherwise have to do themselves. Not time, form, or place utility.

term: Freemium = Two Versions, One Free + One Paid
definition: The freemium business model offers two versions of a product or service: one free (often with ads or limited features) and one paid (premium, ad-free, or full-featured). Example: a music streaming service with a free ad-supported tier and a paid subscription tier.

term: Economic Growth = Domestic Problems More Easily Resolved
definition: When a nation enjoys economic growth, domestic economic problems — such as unemployment, poverty, or budget deficits — are typically more easily resolved. A growing economy generates more tax revenue, employment, and resources to address social challenges.

term: Freedom Limited = Certain Occupations Must Be Licensed
definition: In a private enterprise system, freedom is not unlimited. One example of how freedom is limited: certain occupations must be licensed by the government (e.g., doctors, lawyers, electricians). This ensures minimum competency standards to protect public safety.

term: Economic Service = Ticket to Football Game
definition: An economic service is an intangible activity with monetary value that is bought and sold. A ticket to a football game is an example — it provides entertainment (an intangible experience) in exchange for money. Contrast: goods are tangible; noneconomic services involve no monetary exchange.

term: Capital Goods = Equipment Used in Production
definition: Capital goods are manufactured items used to produce other goods and services. Examples: bulldozers, photocopiers, roads, delivery trucks. Contrast with consumer goods (made for final use), natural resources (raw inputs), and human labor.

A local dry cleaner that launders and presses clothes is selling consumer services — intangible activities performed for consumers, not physical goods. The dry cleaner uses goods (chemicals, machines) but sells a service.

A delivery truck is a capital good — a physical asset used by a business in the production or distribution process. Iron ore (raw material), a truck driver (labor), and weather conditions (natural factor) are NOT capital goods.

One disadvantage of the private enterprise system is the unequal distribution of income. Because businesses and individuals are free to pursue profit and wealth, the system can create significant income inequality. This contrasts with the advantages (competition, innovation, consumer choice).

term: GDP Includes Only Finished Goods = Avoid Double Counting
definition: The Gross Domestic Product (GDP) includes only finished goods and services — not intermediate goods — to avoid double counting. If raw materials and the final product were both counted, the value of inputs would be counted multiple times, inflating the GDP figure.
Owned and operated by one person. Simple to start, full control, but owner is personally liable for all debts.
Two or more people share ownership. General partners have unlimited liability. Limited partners have liability only to their investment.
A legal entity separate from its owners (shareholders). Provides limited liability. Must pay corporate taxes and follow regulations.
Combines partnership flexibility with corporate limited liability. Pass-through taxation.
Passes income, losses, deductions, and credits through to shareholders, avoiding double taxation.
Operates for a purpose other than making profit. Still has social responsibility.
A legally binding agreement. Elements: offer, acceptance, consideration, legality, capacity, and consent.
Something of value exchanged by both parties (money, goods, services, or a promise).
When one party fails to fulfill their obligations. To prove a breach: show the contract existed, was broken, caused financial loss, and the defendant was responsible.
Has no legal effect; was never valid.
Can be cancelled by one party due to fraud, duress, or lack of capacity.

Law created by court decisions rather than written legislation. When the U.S. Supreme Court rules that a business activity is unlawful, it creates common law — binding because no court can overrule the Supreme Court.
A business arrangement requiring authorization from an owner for another entity to use trademarked, copyrighted material (e.g., a university logo on merchandise).

A branch of civil law that addresses civil wrongs — issues that interfere with a person's or firm's rights. Tort law allows injured parties to seek compensation in civil court. Types include negligence (failure of reasonable care), defamation (harming reputation), assault, and conversion. Different from criminal law (where the government prosecutes), contract law, and environmental law.

Law serves several purposes: (1) Maintaining order — laws prohibit violence and harmful behavior to protect society. (2) Enacting social change. (3) Ensuring equality. (4) Resolving disputes. Laws prohibiting violence are an example of the 'maintaining order' function.

A tort (civil wrong) characterized by behavior that drops below a reasonable level of care and harms a person. Example: a company produces an unsafe product for children but fails to include a warning label — the resulting harm is negligence. Different from defamation (harming reputation), assault (threatening/harmful contact), or conversion (unauthorized taking of property).

An arrangement where two or more businesses enter into a relationship by combining complementary resources (technology, skills, capital, distribution) for mutual benefit. Example: two companies execute a joint venture to develop one new product together. Different from a merger (which creates one new company) or a partnership (which is a general business structure).

A business structure with at least one general partner (unlimited liability) and one or more limited partners whose liability is restricted to their investment amount. Limited partners do not participate in day-to-day operations. Best for someone who wants to invest but limit their personal liability.

The plaintiff is the person or party who FILES a lawsuit (brings the case). The defendant is the party being sued. Defendants have the right to present evidence before a court can impose a penalty — this is called procedural due process.

A contract that clearly outlines both sides of an agreement and is enforceable by law. Must have offer, acceptance, consideration, capacity, and legality. Does not have to be written to be valid in all cases.

It IS legal for airlines to overbook flights under U.S. law. Airlines are not required to provide monetary compensation for weather-related delays. Lost baggage compensation amounts vary by situation, not a flat fee. There is no single universal international 'Bill of Rights' for air travelers.

A corporation owned by just a few people (usually family or a small group of founders) that does NOT offer its shares for sale to the general public. Contrast with a public corporation (whose shares trade on a stock exchange).

To attempt to cancel a contract without facing legal action, the appropriate first step is to send a formal request to the other party asking for cancellation. Simply breaching the contract or refusing to pay exposes you to legal liability.
Intangible business assets resulting from thinking. Includes patents, trademarks, and copyrights.
Protects an invention. Grants exclusive rights to make, use, or sell it.
Protects a symbol, word, or phrase identifying a company or product. Can be registered with the government.
Protects original creative works (books, music, art, software).

Confidential business information that gives a company a competitive advantage. To qualify as a trade secret, the information: (1) must be commercially valuable, (2) cannot be known by a large group of people, and (3) must be protected by reasonable security measures (such as a confidentiality agreement). Examples: formulas, recipes, customer lists, business processes.
An organization of workers that negotiates collectively with employers. Advantage for businesses: helps recruit new employees.
Negotiation between employers and union representatives over wages, hours, and conditions.
Controlled by law — a compliance issue for companies. HR must ensure the company pays at least minimum wage.
An employee who reports a business for violating health and safety regulations to a government agency. This is reporting noncompliance and is protected by law.

When two parties in a legal dispute agree to resolve their differences without completing a trial. Settling a case ends the dispute before the end of a trial. It requires BOTH parties to agree, does not require courtroom procedure, and is typically LESS expensive than going to trial.
The process of taking a legal dispute to court. First step: investigating the complaint. Typically more expensive and time-consuming than alternative dispute resolution methods.

An alternative dispute resolution method where a neutral third party helps both sides reach a mutually agreeable solution. Key benefits: cost-effective and efficient. The mediator does not make a binding decision — the parties agree together. Different from arbitration (where the arbitrator makes a binding decision).
A form of alternative dispute resolution where a neutral third party (arbitrator) hears both sides and makes a binding decision. More formal than mediation but less formal than court.
U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission — regulates securities markets and protects investors.
The Federal Reserve Board supervises financial holding companies because they provide a wide variety of financial services.
When a large bank acquires another bank, it gains new customers instantly. This is one of the biggest benefits and motivations for bank mergers.

Joint venture arrangements are typically short-term relationships formed for a specific project or goal. Once the objective is achieved, the venture dissolves. Any type of business structure can benefit from a joint venture — not just large corporations.

Federal statutes are laws enacted by the U.S. Congress (both the House and Senate). Administrative agencies enforce existing statutes; courts interpret them. The Supreme Court rules on constitutionality — but only Congress can create new federal statutes.

Jurisdiction — which court has the authority to hear a case — is an important factor in legal procedure. Without proper jurisdiction, a court cannot legally rule on a case. Jurisdiction is determined by geography, subject matter, and whether the court is state or federal.

A primary responsibility of administrative law is to enforce the regulations created by government agencies (e.g., the EPA, FDA, FTC). Administrative agencies have authority to create rules, investigate violations, and impose penalties within their designated domains.

The litigation process formally begins when the plaintiff files a complaint with the court — not when a settlement is offered, not when an appeal is filed. The complaint states the allegations, and the defendant then receives a summons requiring a response.

Businesses are governed by law at the local, national, and international levels simultaneously. They must comply with city zoning codes, state regulations, federal laws, and international trade agreements all at once.

Local zoning laws affect businesses by restricting the placement of billboards and signage in certain areas. Zoning laws regulate land use generally — they do not prohibit price discrimination, limit monopolies, or require product labels (those are other laws).

A common consequence of a civil lawsuit is paying a monetary fine or damages to the injured party. Civil suits differ from criminal cases: civil outcomes typically involve financial penalties, not imprisonment.

term: Contract Capacity = Sane, Sober, Legal Age
definition: Contract capacity requires that both parties be legally competent to enter an agreement — meaning they must be sane, sober (not under the influence), and of legal age. Without capacity, a contract can be voided.

term: Settlement = Employer Doesn't Expect to Win
definition: An employer is most likely to offer a legal settlement when the employer does not think it will win the case. Settling avoids the risk and cost of an unfavorable court judgment, even if the employer believes it may have some defense.

A business-format franchise provides continuous assistance from the franchisor — including training, operating systems, marketing support, and ongoing guidance. It is the most popular type of franchise. Unlike a product franchise, the entire business model is licensed.

Appropriation is a tort (invasion of privacy) that occurs when a business uses a person's name, image, or likeness for commercial gain without permission. Example: advertising using a celebrity's photo without their consent. The business can be sued for damages.

A bilateral contract exists when both parties make promises to each other. Example: one company promises to deliver 200 units; the other promises to pay $25,000 within 14 days of receipt. Each side has an obligation — making it bilateral.

Antitrust laws protect market competition. Tying agreements — where a supplier requires buyers to purchase one product to get access to another — violate antitrust law because they restrict market competition and buyer choice.

term: Slotting Fees = Ethical Risk When Reducing Competition
definition: Slotting fees (charges paid by manufacturers for shelf space) can become an ethical issue when they reduce competition — for example, by locking out smaller companies who cannot afford the fees, concentrating market power among large corporations.

A franchisee pays an initial fee to the franchisor to open the franchise, and then pays ongoing royalty fees (typically a percentage of revenue or profits). In exchange, the franchisee gets the right to use the brand, systems, and ongoing support.

A controversial legal issue in airport security involves intrusive personal searches — detailed physical pat-downs and full-body imaging. These raise civil liberties concerns about privacy rights versus public safety needs.

Open (public) corporations can be owned by thousands of stockholders through publicly traded shares on a stock exchange. This is a key distinction from close corporations (owned by a small, private group of investors) or sole proprietorships.

term: Clean Water Law = No Toxic Dumping
definition: Clean water laws prohibit businesses from dumping toxic waste or pollutants into rivers, lakes, or other waterways. Violations can result in large fines (e.g., $2 million), criminal charges, and remediation requirements. Different from clean air law or food quality law.
Efficiently organizing data in a database to eliminate redundancy and create a standard format. Goals: eliminate redundant data; ensure data is stored consistently.
Selecting a subset of data for examination. Example: filtering survey results by age to analyze only college students.
Gathering information about external and internal factors affecting a business. Rule: focus only on relevant information to avoid information overload.
CRM data can become inaccurate over time because customer information (addresses, preferences) changes. That's why CRM data cleansing is important.
Uses existing data to describe what happened in the past.
Uses past data to forecast future outcomes and trends.
States that in many real-world data sets, the leading digit is likely to be small (1 appears most often). Used to detect fraud or unusual patterns in financial data.
An umbrella area covering the alignment of business initiatives with IT processes. Key function: ensuring that computer/IT processes work smoothly with business goals.
The unique web address that must be registered. Example: deca.org
The fundamental code that builds web pages. Basic HTML knowledge is essential for creating and maintaining websites.
Processes routine daily business transactions at the operational level. Examples: payroll, hotel reservations, sales orders.
Moving money between accounts electronically. Example: transferring from checking to savings via a banking app.
Used by businesspeople to track appointments and tasks. Mobile devices also allow access to emails, calendars, and more.
Placing quotes around a phrase in a search query finds results containing that exact phrase. Useful for locating specific articles or data.

A line of text at the bottom of a presentation slide. Presenters use footers to include the name, date, and slide numbers for viewer and presenter reference. Different from a header (text at the top) and from animations or transitions.
Giving users only the minimum access needed to perform their job. Reduces the risk of unauthorized access.
Substituting a random number for a sensitive account number during transactions. Financial institutions use it to protect account data.
A fraud where someone pretends to be another person to obtain money or benefits. Firewalls help protect against it.
A basic method businesses use to reduce unauthorized access to confidential information.
Manages money supply and interest rates. May lower interest rates to stimulate a sluggish economy.
Can be used at any business affiliated with the network (Visa, Mastercard). Unlike retail or petroleum cards limited to specific stores.

The first step in designing a multi-table query is to pinpoint EXACTLY what you want to know. If you aren't sure of the precise question, the database cannot provide the right answer. All other steps (determining criteria, locating tables, setting join direction) follow from knowing what question you're asking.

The conditions that filter which records a database retrieves. Criteria should directly match the question being asked. Example: to find high-volume customers in Springdale, use 'Springdale address' AND 'purchases over $100,000' as criteria.
In a multi-table query, the direction in which tables are linked determines how records are matched across tables. Must be set correctly after the question and criteria are determined.

The least expensive way to approach a marketing research project. Internal secondary data is data already collected by the business for other purposes (sales records, expense reports, inventory data). Collecting this data costs little because it already exists. Primary data (new research) costs more.

Data mining involves finding patterns in large datasets. It can also detect anomalies — deviations from standard behavior. Example: a ski resort mining its database detects an anomaly on a holiday weekend when food service decreased even though the resort was at full capacity.
A technology that creates tangible, physical prototypes and products by building them layer by layer from digital designs. Used in product development to quickly create physical models.

Primary research (collecting new data directly) is the appropriate choice when all secondary research options have already been exhausted. Example: an airline executive who has already used all available secondary sources should then conduct primary research to investigate a sales problem.

Qualitative data (interviews, focus groups, open-ended responses) is valuable for rich insights but has a key limitation: it usually only includes a small portion of the target market, making it difficult to generalize to the entire population.

A creative-thinking technique developed by Edward de Bono that stimulates idea generation by asking group members to think from six different perspectives (represented by different colored 'hats'): logic, emotion, caution, optimism, creativity, and process.

A change in the characteristics of a population that affects businesses. Example: the migration of people from one location to another changes the types of products that businesses in those areas need to sell.

A questionnaire design approach that places very broad, general questions at the beginning and progressively moves to narrower, more specific questions toward the end. Helps respondents ease into the survey before tackling detailed topics.

Software that combines multiple business functions (word processing, spreadsheets, database, presentations) into one system. Key benefit: it allows businesses to manage all aspects of the operation from a single platform.

Pre-designed slide layouts in presentation software (like PowerPoint) that include standardized formatting, colors, and placeholder positions. Users select a template and fill in appropriate text and graphics for each slide.

Digital tools that display key performance indicators (KPIs) and other critical business metrics in real time. Businesses use dashboards to quickly track and monitor performance against goals without running individual reports.

A type of marketing research used to collect information from various sources without spending a lot of time or money. Best approach when a company needs to quickly assess whether to pursue a new direction (e.g., international expansion) without committing to expensive primary research.

The activity of transforming raw facts and figures into a useful, organized format that can be used for decision-making. Data processing involves collecting, organizing, and converting raw data into meaningful information. Different from data mining (finding patterns) or information gathering (collecting data).

An operating system that allows many users to access the same programs on different computer units simultaneously throughout a network. Enables shared access to applications and resources across an organization.

A data mining technique that identifies relationships between variables in large datasets. Structure: IF [antecedent] THEN [consequent]. Example: 'If a customer buys peanut butter (antecedent), they are 70% likely to also buy jelly (consequent).'

A type of computer software (not hardware) that uses a knowledge base of gathered expertise to help users make decisions or diagnose problems. It reinforces choices by applying rules derived from human expertise.
A computer-based time-management tool that helps employees keep track of appointments, tasks, and activities. Different from a perpetual calendar, instant-messaging program, or alarm clock.

A controversial method of collecting data on consumer purchasing habits and behavior — tracking users' online activity without their explicit knowledge. Used to assess advertising effectiveness and purchasing patterns, but raises privacy concerns.

The primary reason businesses establish specifications for selecting hardware and software systems is to obtain systems that perform the required functions for their business needs — not just to follow trends or train employees.

One strategy to make data change easier and reduce employee resistance is to communicate the benefits of the data change to staff. People adopt new systems more readily when they understand how the change will help them.

term: Information Monitoring First Step = Identify Needs
definition: The first step in deciding which information to monitor for marketing decision-making is to identify the information needs of the organization — what questions need answering before you start collecting data.

term: Coding Survey Responses = All Possible Answers
definition: When coding questionnaire responses for analysis, market researchers must identify codes that relate to all possible responses — ensuring that every answer can be categorized and analyzed systematically.

A marketing survey that uses a rating scale (e.g., 1 = worst, 5 = best) is an example of coding — assigning numeric values to responses to enable quantitative analysis of qualitative or ranked preferences.

Ethical information management requires implementing procedures to protect the privacy of survey respondents — keeping responses confidential, not distributing participant contact info without consent, and not using deceptive opt-out processes.

A sampling design where the researcher uses personal judgment to select subjects. A non-probability method — not every population member has an equal selection chance. Other non-probability methods: convenience, snowball.

Selling Under the Guise of Research — an unethical practice where a marketer pretends to be conducting a survey but is actually making a sales pitch. Example: claiming to do research, then transitioning into selling a product.

A statistical phenomenon where a trend appears in multiple groups of data but disappears or reverses when the data is combined. Analyzing data with Simpson's Paradox in mind helps account for unconsidered variables or dimensions that skew overall results.

term: Quality Website Source = Reputable Author Credentials
definition: When evaluating online information quality, the most important factor is the credibility of the author's credentials (expertise, qualifications, affiliation). Recency and URL type are secondary considerations.
A data mining method that places data into naturally occurring groups based on similarities. Often used in market segmentation to identify customer groups with shared characteristics.
Launching a product in a limited area to gauge sales potential is an example of causal research — it tests whether offering the product causes a certain level of sales.
A non-probability sampling technique for hard-to-reach populations. Existing participants recruit future participants, causing the sample to grow organically.
A technology trend enabled by computers and telecommunications that allows employees to work in various locations (home, client sites, remote offices) rather than a central office.

A creative-thinking technique that uses metaphors and analogies to generate novel ideas. It makes the familiar seem strange (to question assumptions) and the strange seem familiar (to make new ideas accessible).

Database management systems reduce information redundancy, control access to information, and allow sharing and backup of data across an organization. Key tools: relational databases, cloud storage, database management software.

When a grocery store tracks customer purchases through loyalty cards, it is collecting big data — large volumes of customer behavior data used to identify patterns, personalize offers, and improve business decisions. Not data encryption, data security, or illegal collection.

term: Leading Question = Government Wastes Taxpayers' Money
definition: A leading question suggests the desired answer. Example: 'Do you agree that the government wastes taxpayers' money by supporting unnecessary programs?' The question assumes waste exists, biasing the response toward agreement. This is a survey-design flaw to avoid.

term: Stating Research Problem = Keeps Research Focused
definition: Clearly stating the research problem at the outset helps keep the marketing research project focused and relevant. Without a defined problem, data collection can drift off-topic, wasting resources on information that doesn't answer the business question.

An ongoing trend that affects how efficiently businesses operate is technology improvements. Advances in software, automation, and connectivity continually change how businesses produce, communicate, and deliver goods and services.

term: Fair Information Practice Principles = Not Enforceable Laws
definition: The Fair Information Practice Principles are guidelines for protecting online privacy — but they are NOT enforceable laws. They establish a voluntary framework for how organizations should handle personal data online, but they do not carry legal penalties for non-compliance.

term: Optical Scanning = Reduce Manual Data Entry
definition: To reduce the time it takes to manually enter marketing-research results into a computer database, many businesses use optical scanning devices — which automatically capture data from forms, barcodes, or documents electronically.

term: Likert Scale = Strongly Agree to Strongly Disagree
definition: A Likert scale is a rating scale used in surveys that measures agreement. Respondents choose from options such as: Strongly Agree, Agree, Neutral, Disagree, Strongly Disagree. Example: rating 'The sales representative was friendly and courteous' on a 5-point Likert scale.

term: Presentation for Young Professionals = Concise Information
definition: When preparing a research presentation for an audience of young professionals, a key adaptation is to incorporate concise information — focused, to-the-point content that respects their time. Avoid cluttered slides or excessive detail.

In addition to being clear, questionnaire wording should be concise (brief, no unnecessary words) and objective (neutral, not suggesting a preferred answer). Wording that is ambiguous, complex, or leading produces unreliable data.

One criterion for evaluating the reliability of an information source is timeliness — whether the data is current and up-to-date. Outdated information can lead to poor business decisions. Other criteria: accuracy, authority, and purpose.

term: Content Calendar = Schedule Posts on Each Platform
definition: To prevent any social media platforms from being neglected, businesses should use a content calendar — a planning tool that schedules what content will be posted on which platform and when, ensuring consistent, coordinated posting.
Relies heavily on expensive equipment and materials. Examples: power plants, automobile factories.
Relies primarily on human workers. Examples: restaurants, handmade crafts.
Determining how products will be produced and in what amounts. Involves figuring out specific needs (resources, timelines, quantities) before production begins.

A production activity that helps businesses decide how much to produce by predicting how much of a product consumers will want to buy in a given period. Businesses use previous sales data and current economic conditions to forecast demand.
Establishes the timetable to be followed in production. Different from forecasting (which determines how much to produce).
Steps: mapping → analyzing → redesigning → acquiring resources → implementing.
Development of something new or improvement of something existing. Example: adding automation to an assembly line.
Using technology to perform tasks with minimal human effort. Reduces labor costs; improves speed and consistency.
A purchasing decision about whether to produce parts in-house or buy them from a supplier. Two key considerations: Can the company make the parts? Would it be cost-effective?
A purchasing process where a buyer sends requests for price quotes to multiple suppliers and then reviews the proposed prices (bids) before selecting the best supplier based on cost or overall terms.

For a business to generate a profit, its operating costs must be LESS THAN the gross margin. Operating costs are subtracted from gross margin to determine net profit. If operating costs equal or exceed gross margin, there is no profit.

When evaluating the performance of distribution channel members, a logistics manager should first consider: cost efficiency (are costs minimized?) and customer service (are customers satisfied?). Credit rating and utility costs are secondary considerations.

Process design can be affected by regulatory factors — government requirements and laws. Example: a manufacturing company required by local government to dispose of waste in an environmentally safe way must design its processes to comply with that regulation.

A production method where the same products are made continuously with no breaks in the process. Best suited for businesses that produce products with steady, predictable demand for large quantities. Examples: oil refining, food processing.

Workplace policies designed to protect employees and customers from harm. Examples: requiring proper equipment operation, keeping aisles clear, posting wet floor signs, providing fire exit information. Government health and safety laws establish minimum requirements.

A condition or situation in the workplace that has the potential to cause injury or harm. Example: a slippery spot where oil was spilled — a potential cause of falls and injury. Businesses are responsible for identifying and correcting hazards.

term: Industrial Purchasing Last Step = Confirm Delivery
definition: In the industrial purchasing process, the final step is confirming delivery — verifying that the goods arrived, in the correct quantity, at the right time. Steps before this include identifying needs, requesting bids, negotiating, and selecting a supplier.

A production method that runs in batches or cycles — stopping and starting rather than running continuously. Best suited for products NOT in constant demand (like seasonal goods or custom orders). Examples: a business making wool coats only in fall, or custom furniture.

term: Fire Prevention = Know Location of Fire Extinguishers
definition: An effective fire-prevention program requires that all employees know the exact location of all fire extinguishers. This is distinct from knowing hazardous materials locations or emergency service contacts.

Businesses that provide services produce intangible outputs — results that cannot be physically touched or stored. Example: a gymnastics studio produces intangible outputs (teaching skills, experiences) rather than physical goods.

A purchase order used for recurring purchases from the same supplier where the item and price are set, but quantity and delivery schedule may vary. Used when a business regularly buys from a trusted vendor at agreed terms.

Formal processes for documenting workplace accidents, near-misses, or safety incidents. A key benefit: when incidents are properly reported, managers can investigate possible causes and implement preventive measures.

Calculated by dividing total output by the number of employees. Formula: Total Output Value ÷ Number of Employees. Measures how much value each worker generates. Example: 4 workers produce $10,000 in goods → $10,000 ÷ 4 = $2,500 per employee.

Work teams made up of members from different departments or functions (marketing, finance, operations, etc.). Businesses use cross-functional teams to recognize product opportunities on a continuous basis, as diverse perspectives lead to better innovation.

Supplies used in the day-to-day operations of a business but not incorporated into the final product. Examples: office supplies like paper, pens, and notepads; cleaning products; printer ink. Businesses keep MRO goods in inventory for regular use.

A Research and Development project involves inventing and testing brand-new technologies or processes that did not previously exist. High risk, high reward. Different from derivative projects (improving existing products) and platform projects (creating new product families from existing technology).
Tracking the amount, kind, and value of items a business possesses. Goal: maximize inventory to avoid stockouts while minimizing storage costs.
Buying items (furniture, supplies, uniforms) for the company's own use — not for resale.
Comparing prices offered by different vendors for the same goods or services to decide from whom to purchase.
A problem-solving group of employees who study workplace problems and recommend solutions to management.
An environment where all employees are committed to continuous improvement. Everyone's ideas are valued.
The lowest form of interdependence. Departments operate with little to no interaction. Each department impacts the overall organization but departments rarely coordinate directly.

One department's output becomes another department's input — work flows in one direction. Example: a fabric buyer (Bill) and a small-items buyer (Tara) must supply materials to the sewer (Lauren) before she can do her job. Lauren needs denim from Bill and thread/buttons/zippers from Tara.
Departments work back and forth with each other, each relying on the other's output. The highest form of interdependence.
Emphasizes delegation and is flexible; employees share responsibilities. Primary characteristic: delegation emphasis.
Conducted when an employee leaves the company. Purpose: gauge employee attitudes, gain improvement suggestions, and understand turnover. Different from onboarding activities.
A common type of injury caused by improper lifting of heavy or bulky objects. Back strains often take a long time to heal and may never heal completely. Proper lifting technique prevents this.

Identify the project's stakeholders FIRST — before determining quality standards, identifying resources, or developing a draft plan. Stakeholders must be included from the start so their input and perspectives shape all other planning steps.

All individuals or groups involved in or affected by a project. Must be identified at the beginning of project planning because you do not want to start any other aspect of planning without getting their input.
First step: define the question or problem clearly before generating ideas.

A creative thinking technique where a group identifies as many different ideas as possible during a set time frame. Key rules: (1) All ideas are equal — even outlandish ones. (2) Building on others' ideas is encouraged (called stepping-stones or triggers). (3) Ideas should not be evaluated or criticized during the session. (4) More ideas are better. Brainstorming is NOT considered stealing when someone builds on another person's idea.
Drawing a visual map of ideas to see how one idea relates to another. Different from brainstorming.
Picturing something in your mind. Different from brainstorming.
Acting out a situation. Different from brainstorming.
A group formed specifically to discuss a topic in depth. Efficient way to get a range of opinions quickly.
Assigning tasks to others. Essential for managers — one person cannot do everything alone.

Regularly checking project progress saves time and resources that might otherwise be wasted. Example: catching an error early (needing three sources instead of two) meant only rewriting the introduction — not the entire paper. Monitoring is not the same as evaluating project success.

Reviewing outcomes after a project concludes to determine whether goals were achieved and to gather stakeholder feedback. Communicating with stakeholders about their satisfaction builds relationships and makes them more likely to participate in future projects.
A plan for moving products back through the supply chain — managing product returns, repairs, recycling, or disposal. Developed when a business needs to revamp its product return system.

Reviewing and analyzing purchase transaction data to evaluate sales performance. Example: a manager reviewing a report comparing purchase transactions of the company's four largest customers is conducting a sales analysis.

Ordering additional inventory because current supply ran short. Example: a company ran out of paper towels mid-month and orders four additional cases — this is a reorder, not a new product order or a custom order.

Improves service and reduces cost by aligning purchasing, production, warehousing, inventory, and transportation into a comprehensive, optimized system. The goal is to coordinate all five elements to minimize cost and maximize service levels.

The tax code is part of a business's external environment — an outside factor the business does not control but must respond to. Other external factors: competition, economy, regulation. Contrast: internal factors (marketing, R&D, personnel) are within business control.

term: Quality Management Plan = Deliverables + The Project Itself
definition: A project's quality-management plan includes strategies to ensure the quality of both the deliverables (the project outputs) AND the quality of the project itself (how the work is managed and executed). Not just one or the other.

'Big Q' is another name for Total Quality Management (TQM) — a comprehensive, organization-wide approach to continuous quality improvement. Contrast with 'little q' (narrow, product-only quality focus). TQM involves all employees and all processes.

Small-business owners develop operating procedures so that everyday tasks are performed in a standard, consistent way. When Luke follows the steps to complete and submit an expense-reimbursement form (with receipts and manager approval), he is following company procedures.

term: Determine HR Needs = List All Tasks First
definition: To determine human resource needs for a new business, the most practical first step is to list all the tasks involved in operating the business. Once you know all the work that must be done, you can determine how many people with which skills are needed.

When traveling internationally for business, a key document to bring is a business visa — a permit issued by the destination country that allows entry specifically for business purposes. Different from a tourist visa, which does not authorize business activities.

term: Poor Presentation = Reflects on Company Image
definition: When an employee gives a poor presentation (arrives late, fails to prepare, performs badly), it reflects poorly on the company's image — not just the individual. Clients and partners associate the employee's performance with the organization as a whole.

term: Proactive Management = Planning in Advance for Change
definition: Proactive management involves planning in advance for change — anticipating future challenges and preparing strategies before problems arise. Contrast: reactive management (responding after problems occur). Proactive is always the preferred approach.

Companies develop positive organizational cultures because positive culture is directly linked to improved results — higher employee engagement, better customer service, lower turnover, and stronger financial performance.

term: Matrix Organizational Design = Shares Human Resources
definition: One reason businesses use a matrix organizational design is to share human resources across different projects or departments simultaneously. In a matrix structure, employees report to both a functional manager and a project manager.
Strategies, processes, technology, and people a business uses to build and maximize long-term customer value.

Software that manages interactions with customers and potential customers. Used to store information such as sales opportunities so that employees can turn those opportunities into buying customers. Does NOT typically store job applications, product inventory, or budget spreadsheets — those are unrelated to customer management.

A CRM system keeps records of a customer's sales history, including their prior purchases. This information helps a company determine a customer's product preferences. A customer's name, address, or preferred payment method provides very few clues about product preferences.
Used to segment customers, analyze purchasing patterns, and evaluate customer value. Helps businesses better understand their customers.
A type of CRM technology that supports, coordinates, and increases the productivity of sales professionals.
The process of updating and correcting customer data. Important because CRM data is 'perishable' — it becomes inaccurate over time.
A business's contributions to charity. Part of overall social responsibility.

Employees should take responsibility to find out what they need to know to do their jobs well. The primary reason employees learn as much as possible about the business and its products is to answer customers' inquiries — not to create publicity, assist coworkers, or reduce promotional budgets.

A customer whose behavior fluctuates — sometimes friendly, sometimes snapping or difficult. Employees should remain patient and treat these customers consistently and professionally, just as they would any other customer.

term: Customer Service Mindset and Career Advancement
definition: Having a customer service mindset also benefits employees personally — it helps them advance in their careers by demonstrating professionalism, reliability, and commitment to the organization's goals.

Consistently enforcing return policies benefits the business by reducing misunderstandings, ill will, and inconsistent treatment of customers. When exceptions are made arbitrarily, it can create resentment among customers who are treated differently.
