The travel and tourism industry provides services including: airlines, cruise ships, trains, buses, car rentals, lodging (hotels), and conventions/events. These are the core service categories that make travel possible for consumers.

A touchpoint that occurs while the customer is actively making a purchase. Example: the physical aspects of the business (store layout, atmosphere, signage). Contrast with pre-sale touchpoints (advertising, reviews) and post-sale touchpoints (follow-up emails, warranties).

One way to meet customer needs without always asking is to get to know customers personally — learning their preferences, habits, and past purchases. This proactive approach allows employees to offer personalized service before the customer has to ask.

term: Store of Value (in a business context)
definition: Money functions as a store of value when it is saved rather than spent — it retains value for future purchasing power.

Being present at work when scheduled is fundamental to being a good employee. Plan vacation time wisely so you are not absent at crucial times for your team. Even being 10 minutes late daily adds up significantly over time.
Two guidelines: maintain perspective (keep your role in mind) and show respect for others.

Creating positive customer relations often involves taking practical action to help a customer. Example: when a customer is struggling to carry multiple packages, offering to place all packages in one large bag is a simple, practical way to build goodwill — not just verbal thanks or compliments.

term: Responding to Customer Inquiry = Accurate Backorder Info
definition: When responding to a customer inquiry, the appropriate action is to give accurate, helpful information. Example: informing a customer that a product is currently on backorder but expected within the week is a proper response to a product-availability inquiry.

Reinforcing service orientation through communication benefits the business by building positive relationships with customers. Consistent, helpful communication establishes trust and customer loyalty over time.

When a company eliminates its employee training program to cut costs, a likely negative consequence is decreased quality of customer service. Undertrained employees are less equipped to handle customer needs, resolve complaints, and represent the brand well.

term: Positive Moment of Truth = Stranded Passengers
definition: A 'moment of truth' is any customer-business interaction that shapes the customer's impression. A positive moment of truth during a crisis (e.g., cancelled flight) would be proactively providing complimentary hotel rooms and food for stranded passengers — exceeding expectations.

term: Respond to Pricing Inquiry = Check Computer
definition: When a customer asks about a product's price and the employee doesn't immediately know it, the correct response is to check the price in the computer and give an accurate answer — not to guess or give an approximate price.

term: End Calls Pleasantly = Good Last Impression
definition: All calls coming into a business should end as pleasantly as possible in order to leave a good last impression with callers. The last thing a customer experiences in a call shapes their overall perception of the business.

A product-related complaint involves issues with the product itself — not the service or personnel. Example: a customer receiving an incorrect size is a product complaint. Contrast: pressure selling (selling practice complaint) or poor service (personnel complaint).

Consumers of hospitality products have the right to expect businesses to provide goods and services that are safe and clean. This is a baseline standard — not guaranteed satisfaction, multiple amenities, or meeting each individual's personal standards.

A complaint caused by an employee's behavior — particularly rudeness — is classified as a personnel complaint. Other types: product complaints (defects), service complaints (slow or poor delivery), and pricing complaints (unexpected charges).

term: Diffuse Customer Anger = Listen, Breathe, Apologize
definition: When faced with an angry customer, the best approach is to take a deep breath, listen to the complaint, and apologize for the inconvenience. This acknowledges the customer's feelings without escalating the situation or becoming defensive.

term: Reinforce Company Image = Communicate Service Standards
definition: To reinforce the company's customer-friendly image, managers should consistently communicate the company's service-quality expectations to employees. Employees who understand expectations can deliver consistent service that matches the brand promise.

term: Social Media Positive Review = Respond Quickly
definition: When a business receives a positive review on social media, the best response is to respond quickly — acknowledging the customer's feedback and expressing appreciation. This enhances the customer's experience, builds loyalty, and demonstrates active engagement.

term: Develop Repeat Business = Offer New Service
definition: One effective way to develop repeat business and build customer loyalty is to introduce a new service that adds value. This gives existing customers a reason to return and shows that the business is evolving to meet their needs.

term: Be Helpful = Phone Supervisor for Customer Answer
definition: One technique for building positive customer relations is to be helpful — even when you don't know the answer. The appropriate action is to immediately phone a supervisor or resource to get the correct information for the customer, rather than guessing or deflecting.

Providing efficient follow-up — such as promptly calling a customer back with a status update — reinforces the company's image for reliability and customer care. Customers appreciate when employees take ownership of issues and follow through.

term: Responding to Inquiry = Call Back by End of Day
definition: When a customer requests information and the employee needs time to research it, the professional response is to commit to a specific timeline — such as calling back by end of day. This sets clear expectations and demonstrates reliability.

term: Resolve Customer Service = Let Customer Express Feelings
definition: A good strategy for resolving a customer service issue is to allow the customer to fully express his or her feelings before responding. Letting customers vent defuses tension and shows empathy — before moving into problem-solving.

When a guest speaker is running late but still plans to arrive before his or her scheduled presentation, the best contingency approach is to arrange a video conference call in case the speaker is delayed further — ensuring continuity for the audience.

term: Success or Failure Depends on Customer Service
definition: In the hospitality and tourism industry, business success or failure directly depends on customer service. A single poor experience can drive customers away permanently. Superior service is the primary differentiator in this relationship-driven sector.
Communicates the employer's philosophy and expectations. Sets the tone for the new hire's experience with the company.
HR tasks performed when a new employee joins — ensuring payroll setup, introducing to colleagues, assigning a mentor.
When HR executives participate in long-range planning, they are engaged in strategic management.

Human resources professionals foster fairness in the hiring process by using the same process consistently for ALL applicants — all applications and interviews handled the same way. Illegal practices: asking applicants to reveal marital status, implementing racial screening, or refusing to interview candidates with certain backgrounds.

The study of the type of duties involved in a particular job and the skills a person needs to perform it. Retailers and businesses conduct a job analysis BEFORE selecting new employees to ensure they hire the most qualified candidates. Different from a job description (outlines responsibilities) or job application (form filled out by applicant).

When evaluating employee performance, businesses consider work habits (dependability, initiative, showing up on time, completing tasks without constant supervision). Education is considered during hiring, not evaluation. Seniority is considered for promotions. Hourly wages are not an evaluation factor.

A positive, fact-finding approach to addressing an employee's problematic behavior. The general intent is to identify the issue objectively and help the employee improve — not to punish. Done constructively to support behavior change while maintaining dignity.
Assesses current and future human resources needs by examining how much work must be done and what staffing levels are required. Used in workforce planning.

term: Equal Pay for Men and Women
definition: A basic employee right: men and women must receive equal pay for equal work. This is protected by law (e.g., the Equal Pay Act in the U.S.). Unequal pay based on gender is illegal discrimination.

A common onboarding approach where a new employee trains by working alongside an experienced colleague for a period of time (a transition period). Allows the new hire to learn job duties in context before working independently.
Identifying customer needs and creating products/services to meet them. One of the six primary business activities.
Obtaining, developing, maintaining, and improving a product mix in response to market opportunities. A marketing function — includes calculating costs of products.
Informs customers about new products, improved products, new uses, and special values. One of the seven marketing functions.
Customer orientation — finding out what a buyer wants and providing it. Not convincing a buyer to buy what the company has.
A section of a business plan that provides a comprehensive description of the customers the new business wants to attract — demographics, buying patterns, geographic area, and family characteristics.

Groups of consumers who share similar characteristics (demographics, lifestyles, interests, needs). Businesses identify market segments to tailor their products and marketing efforts. Segments are formed by dividing consumers into groups with similar characteristics.
A strategy used for outdated or declining products where the business stops investing in the product and instead extracts maximum remaining profit before eventually discontinuing it.

An advertising strategy that allows a business to select and reach qualified prospects — specific individuals or groups who are likely to be interested in the product. More targeted than mass advertising.
Setting a low price to get a product into more customers' hands and gain market share quickly. Used as a pricing objective when the goal is broad customer adoption.
During the maturity stage, a company's promotional activities focus on pointing out the differences between its product and competing products, as competition intensifies and the market is saturated.
Setting prices at the same level as those charged by comparable businesses in the industry. Based on industry standards rather than target markets, channel members, or product life cycles.

A channel management task where a business shares the cost of producing promotional materials with product manufacturers. Both benefit: the business gets help paying for advertising; manufacturers get increased product visibility.
PR activities use communication channels to provide information to the public — not primarily to target customers, advertise an image, or market products. The primary function is information sharing.

A business that purchases goods for use in manufacturing or producing other goods — not for resale to consumers. Example: a construction company buying wood, or a car manufacturer buying steel. Different from retailers (who sell to consumers) and wholesalers (who sell to retailers).

A distribution strategy that places a product in as many outlets as possible. Used for convenience goods and new consumer products that need maximum availability. Contrast: selective (limited outlets) and exclusive (very few, often luxury).

One key purpose of developing a marketing plan is that it provides guidelines for all personnel to understand how to meet the company's marketing objectives. Without a plan, employees lack direction for their day-to-day activities.

Intermediaries (wholesalers, distributors) buy large quantities of goods from producers and sell smaller quantities to other intermediaries or consumers. By buying in bulk, they reduce the per-unit cost for goods throughout the supply chain.

A public relations tool containing information about a company for use by media. Typically includes biographies of key company figures, background information, press releases, and fact sheets. Used to introduce the company to journalists and media outlets.

A form of sales promotion where a company's product or brand is featured within a video game. Example: a company releases a video game where characters use or prominently display a certain brand of equipment.

term: Cable TV Advertising = Select Special Markets
definition: A benefit of using cable television for promotions is that it provides access to a select group of stations that reach specific, targeted market segments. Cable channels often cater to niche audiences.

An advantage of using the internet for direct marketing is that it is relatively affordable compared to traditional forms of direct marketing (print, mail, personal selling). It also reaches a broad audience and allows easy tracking.

A profit-oriented pricing objective focuses on financial return. Key types: earning a target return on investment (ROI), maximizing profit, or achieving a specific profit margin. Different from sales-oriented (increase market share) or status quo (match competitors) pricing objectives.

A statement that clearly communicates what makes a business or product stand out from competitors — what unique value it offers that others don't. Example: 'We are the most affordable gym in town!' emphasizes a pricing advantage as the USP.
Setting objectives, analyzing the competitive environment, and making high-level decisions. One of six primary business activities.
Evaluates Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats.
A strategic planning framework with seven elements: Strategy, Structure, Systems, Shared Values, Skills, Staff, and Style.
Usually responsible for setting policy — the process of governing the business.

Businesses track the performance of a business plan by analyzing the effectiveness of their operating strategies — evaluating whether planned approaches are actually working and generating expected results.

Giving more decision-making authority to lower levels of the organization. Example: giving more buying responsibility to local stores that are part of large retail chains. Opposite: centralization (decisions made at the top).

term: HR Changes Ripple Throughout the Business
definition: When a company experiences a change in human resources management — such as a new hiring process, compensation structure, or training program — it will also experience changes throughout the entire organization, including financial analysis, operations, and company culture.
The organizing management function establishes a company's levels of authority — defining who reports to whom and how decision-making power is distributed throughout the organization.

Effective leaders recognize that most goals can't be reached alone and willingly share their power and authority with others. Sharing power builds trust, develops other leaders, and enables the organization to accomplish more collectively.

The directing management function is a continuous process — not a one-time event. Managers must provide ongoing guidance, motivation, and instruction to employees throughout operations to keep work aligned with goals.

term: Managerial Planning = Prepare for the Future
definition: The primary purpose of managerial planning is to prepare for the future — anticipating what the organization will need to do and how it will accomplish its objectives. Planning enables businesses to be proactive rather than reactive.

term: Honesty About Time Commitment in Hiring
definition: Entrepreneurs must be honest about the time and effort required when interviewing potential employees. Sugarcoating leads to employee resentment and high turnover. Employees hired under false expectations will feel overburdened and are unlikely to stay. There is no law limiting workers to 40 hours per week — honesty is the primary reason for transparency in hiring.
If customers are not satisfied with project work, you may need to redo the project work or complete another project to satisfy them.
Beginning securities brokers (sales agents) are required to pass a licensing exam to sell securities before they can work in the field.

An analysis conducted to determine whether a business idea is viable. Completed BEFORE the business plan is developed. Examines whether the concept can be executed and whether it is likely to succeed. Each feasibility study may focus on one potential business model. If similar businesses already exist, a feasibility study is still valuable.

In addition to financial risks, entrepreneurs face personal and emotional risks including: marital tension, family problems, and the psychological stress of uncertainty and long hours. These are distinct from financial risks (uncertain income) and economic risks (inflation, competition).

When selecting capital resources (equipment), a way to save money is to look for used equipment rather than always buying new. Buying equipment is generally preferable to renting if the business plans to use it long-term.

Exaggerated, over-the-top claims about a product or business that go beyond the facts. Example: an entrepreneur exaggerating year-one income to attract investors. Different from outright fraud, but still misleading and unethical. In advertising, puffery (vague superlatives) is generally legal but specific false claims are not.
The stage of the entrepreneurial process when the entrepreneur determines the amount of capital needed to launch the business, develops the business plan, and secures funding.
The costs required to register a new business as a corporation with the government. Also known as startup registration costs or establishment fees.

Entrepreneurs should always evaluate the potential impact of new technologies before applying them to business practices. Not every new technology is right for every business — the key question is whether the benefits outweigh the costs and disruption.

The first step in entrepreneurial discovery is identifying an unmet want or need in the marketplace. Before analyzing feasibility, determining risks, or screening product ideas, the entrepreneur must first spot a gap or opportunity.

One of the most important habits of successful entrepreneurs is careful observation of their environment, customers, and markets. Observing trends, problems, and behaviors helps entrepreneurs spot new product and business ideas.

Technological trends — like the growth of cloud computing applications — create new business opportunities and impact how new ventures operate. Entrepreneurs must stay current with technology trends to identify opportunities and risks.

A startup founder should consider hiring employees when they are turning down work due to limited capacity — meaning the business has more demand than it can handle. Not when stressed, uncertain, or just getting started.

term: Use Resources Productively — Resources are Limited
definition: New businesses should use their resources productively because resources (money, time, equipment, people) are often limited. Efficient use of limited resources is critical to survival and growth.

term: Exit Strategy — Plan When Writing Business Plan
definition: Entrepreneurs should start thinking about their exit strategy when writing the initial business plan — not after losing money, not after finding a buyer, and not after leaving. Planning ahead ensures the business is built in a way that supports eventual exit goals.

term: Business Running Successfully — Next Step = Expand
definition: When a business has been running successfully for several years, a likely next financial step is expansion — such as purchasing a second location. This is distinct from startup phases (acquiring funds, setting goals) which come earlier.

term: Product Opportunity from Changing Life Circumstances
definition: Entrepreneurs identify new product opportunities by recognizing how changing life circumstances create unmet needs. Example: a college graduate needing professional clothes — a product opportunity resulting from a life change.

term: Group Idea Generation = Variety of Perspectives
definition: The advantage of generating product ideas as a group is that more participants provide a variety of perspectives, leading to a broader, richer idea set than any one person could generate alone.

term: Venture Creation First Step = Conduct Research
definition: When determining venture-creation opportunities, entrepreneurs must first conduct research — gathering information about the market, competition, and customer needs before committing resources.

A career in entrepreneurship is characterized by being innovative (creating new solutions), risky (uncertain outcomes), and time-intensive (requiring long, irregular hours). Contrast: routine, predictable, low-risk careers.

term: Feasibility Study Technical Section = Where Work Happens
definition: The technical or logistical section of a feasibility study includes information about WHERE the work will happen — the physical location, facilities, equipment, and operational logistics required.
An important question about timing a new product release: Is the market willing AND able to pay for it? Both willingness (desire) and ability (financial capacity) are required for a viable market.

term: Increased Market Share = Gains Ground on Competitors
definition: When a business improves products, marketing, or customer service to capture a larger portion of the total available market, it is gaining ground on competitors through increased market share.

A goal of business sustainability is maximizing long-term profitability — not short-term profits at the expense of future viability. Sustainable businesses balance financial performance with environmental and social responsibilities.

term: Innovative Entrepreneurship = Creating Something Genuinely New
definition: Truly innovative entrepreneurial endeavors involve developing and selling something genuinely new — like a new device or technology. Buying an existing business or opening a franchise is less entrepreneurially innovative.

term: Consult Industry Expert When Lacking Knowledge
definition: An entrepreneur is most likely to discuss a new concept with an industry expert when they lack knowledge or experience in the field — not when seeking loans, increasing margins, or planning promotions.

term: Poor Communication Reduces New Business Success
definition: Poor communication reduces employee productivity and has a negative effect on new business success. Clear communication is essential for coordinating operations, setting expectations, and building culture.
A characteristic of an efficient accounting system is that it is user-friendly — easy for employees to learn and use correctly. Other characteristics: accurate, accessible, and results-oriented.
Many computerized accounting systems make up-to-the-minute data available to financial decision-makers instantaneously — providing real-time data for timely decisions.

A possible downside of obtaining investment from venture capitalists is sharing company ownership. Unlike debt financing (loans), equity investment from VCs gives them a stake in the business and a voice in decisions.

An accounting method that records revenues and expenses when they occur (when earned or incurred), not when cash actually changes hands. Contrast with cash accounting (records only when cash is received or paid).

term: Personal Net Worth = Assets Minus Debts
definition: Personal net worth is calculated by selling all of your assets and paying off all your debts — what remains is your net worth. Formula: Net Worth = Total Assets − Total Liabilities.

term: Pay Credit Card in Full = Avoid Interest
definition: You should pay your credit card bill in full each month to avoid accumulating interest charges. When you pay only the minimum, the remaining balance accrues interest, increasing your total cost.

A 401(k) is a defined contribution plan — an employer-sponsored retirement account where employees contribute a portion of their paycheck (often with employer matching). The final benefit depends on contributions and investment performance.

An externality is a cost or benefit that affects a third party not directly involved in a transaction. An unquantifiable liability — one that cannot be precisely measured in dollar amounts — is a type of externality.

term: Internal Control Procedures = Reduce Unrecorded Sales
definition: Businesses develop internal control procedures to reduce the possibility of employees accepting payment without recording the sale — preventing theft, fraud, and accounting errors.

A financial tool that entrepreneurs and managers use to plan, monitor, and manage their venture's income and expenses over a set period. Different from a capital budget (for long-term investments) or cash flow statement (actual cash movement).
Simple interest = Principal × Rate × Time. Example: $3,000 at 4.25% for 2 years = $3,000 × 0.0425 × 2 = $255 in interest. Total repayment = $3,000 + $255 = $3,255.

term: First Step in Personal Budget = Establish Goals
definition: The first step in developing a personal budget is to establish your financial goals — what you want to achieve with your money. This gives the budget purpose and direction before you estimate income or track expenses.

The original records that provide evidence of a financial transaction. Examples: checks, receipts, invoices, and purchase orders. Source documents are the foundation of the accounting system and provide proof for every entry.
An advantage to retailers of offering credit is that credit customers often buy impulse items more freely — not limited by cash on hand, they add unplanned purchases more easily.

term: Accounting Software = Create Invoices, Record Payments
definition: Accounting software programs increase efficiency by creating invoices and recording customer payments. Distinct from payroll software, presentation tools, or market-share dashboards.
Monthly payments protecting a home from natural disasters or damage are insurance premiums — transferring the financial risk of property damage to the insurer.

term: Progressive Tax = Higher Income, Higher Rate
definition: In a progressive tax system, individuals with higher incomes pay a larger percentage in taxes. Contrast: flat tax (same rate for all), regressive tax (heavier burden on lower incomes).
Finance charges on credit accounts are expressed as an annual percentage rate (APR). APR allows consumers to compare the true cost of credit across different products.
A cosigner is legally responsible for repaying a bank loan if the primary borrower fails to make payments. Banks require cosigners when borrowers have insufficient credit history or income.

A business adjusts its budget figures in response to changing economic trends — recession reducing demand, inflation raising costs, or a boom creating new opportunities. Economic conditions directly affect revenue and expense projections.
A voluntary debtor-creditor relationship is one both parties choose to enter. Classic example: obtaining a loan from a bank — the borrower agrees to repay the lender with interest.

Saving (savings accounts, CDs) helps achieve short-term financial goals within a few years. Investing (stocks, bonds, mutual funds) helps achieve long-term wealth-building goals. Investing carries more risk but potentially higher return.

The biggest risk in real estate investment is illiquidity — the inability to quickly convert the property to cash. Unlike stocks, real estate can take months to sell and may require a price discount in urgent situations.
Gross Profit = Net Sales − Cost of Goods Sold. Larry's Pro Shop: $583,000 − $259,000 = $324,000 gross profit. Net profit ($100,000 after taxes) is a separate figure.

term: Private Equity = No Repayment, Share Profits
definition: An entrepreneur who does not want the legal obligation of repaying a loan and is willing to share profits selects private equity. Equity investors receive an ownership stake and share of profits — not loan repayment.

A debtor-creditor relationship exists when one party owes money to another and interest is charged on the balance. A sale paid in full or a stock transaction does not constitute a debtor-creditor relationship.
When customers pay outstanding balances, the business records cash received and reduces the accounts receivable balance — the amount customers no longer owe.
Non-corporations (partnerships, S corps, sole proprietorships) are pass-through entities — profits and losses flow to the owners' personal tax returns, not taxed at the corporate level.
When an entrepreneur gets a commercial bank loan to finance a new venture, the bank typically requires collateral — assets pledged as security against the loan in case of default.

term: Willingness to Take Risks = Open Business Despite Chance of Failure
definition: A defining entrepreneurial trait is the willingness to take risks. Example: Sidney intends to open a sports memorabilia shop even though there is a significant chance it will fail — he exhibits willingness to take risks, not creativity or organization.

The best example of an entrepreneurial venture is one that involves creating something genuinely new — like a veterinarian inventing a device to solve a problem. Buying a franchise, adding menu items, or holding seasonal events are less entrepreneurially innovative.

A global trend that encourages entrepreneurs to consider international business opportunities is lower tariffs — reduced taxes on imported/exported goods make international trade more accessible and profitable for small businesses.

term: Critical Startup Equipment = Printing Machine, T-Shirts, Artwork
definition: For a home-based T-shirt printing business, the most critical equipment and supplies are: T-shirts (the product), a printing machine (the production tool), and artwork (the design input). Display cases and thread are less critical to getting started.

term: Best-Equipped Entrepreneurs = Passion for Their Work
definition: The entrepreneurs best equipped to work long hours and overcome obstacles are ones who possess passion for their work. Passion sustains motivation through the inevitable challenges of building a business.

term: Startup Financial Needs = Depend on Nature of Venture
definition: The financial needs to start a new business depend on the nature of the venture — its industry, scale, location, and operational model. There is no universal startup cost; requirements vary enormously across business types.

term: Talk with Industry Experts = Provides Useful Information
definition: Entrepreneurs should talk with industry experts when developing new business concepts because industry experts typically provide useful information — practical knowledge about the market, operations, and challenges that saves time and prevents costly mistakes.

When an entrepreneur seeks a high profit from a new venture, the risk is usually higher. The risk-reward relationship is fundamental in business: higher potential returns require accepting greater uncertainty and exposure to loss.

term: Social Risk = Long Hours, Less Time with Friends and Family
definition: Starting a new business involves social risk — personal and relationship costs. Example: working 14 hours a day and not having time to spend with friends or family. Social risk differs from financial risk (losing money) and physical risk (health effects).

A human resources strategy where employees rotate through different job positions or work stations. Benefits: builds versatility, prevents burnout, and cross-trains employees. Example: manager trainees spend two weeks at each work station — grill, register, drive-thru.

When a manager recognizes that an employee is demonstrating racial or ethnic bias (e.g., delegating only to certain races), the most effective approach is to address the behavior privately and directly with the employee.
A primary factor that small-business owners should consider when deciding to hire additional staff is payroll expenses — whether the business can sustain the additional labor cost.
A work-based program that helps employees address personal and professional issues. Services often include career and education planning, counseling, financial advice, and mental health support.
Before a training session begins, providing an overview lets staff members know what to expect during training. This reduces anxiety, helps employees prepare, and increases training effectiveness.

term: Remedial Action = Resume Normal Attitude Quickly
definition: When administering remedial (corrective) action to an employee, a supervisor should resume a normal attitude toward the employee as quickly as possible after the action — avoiding ongoing hostility or awkwardness.
To prevent understaffing during busy periods (peaks in demand), businesses should proactively increase staffing levels — hiring seasonal workers, scheduling extra shifts, or cross-training employees.

Businesses establish firm policies on discriminatory behavior to promote diversity and ensure all employees are treated fairly. These policies protect against discriminatory treatment based on race, gender, age, religion, etc.

term: Need More Staff = Consistently Missing Deadlines
definition: A sign that a business needs to hire more employees is that it is often unable to meet its deadlines — meaning the current workforce is overextended and workload exceeds capacity.

The main purpose of encouraging feedback during a training session is to ensure employees understand the material. Feedback reveals gaps in comprehension so trainers can clarify and reinforce key concepts.

A corrective approach to managing employee rule violations is intended to discourage further violations of company rules. Unlike punitive termination, corrective action focuses on changing behavior going forward.

A sales forecasting method where company executives use their collective judgment to estimate future sales. Advantages: quick and easy to use, leverages internal expertise. Disadvantage: subjective, can be swayed by dominant personalities.

A strategy that focuses marketing efforts on a very specific, well-defined segment of the market rather than the broader market. Example: an athletic footwear company producing shoes specifically for children who play soccer.

term: Marketing Strategies Can Be Changed as Needed
definition: Marketing strategies are not permanent — they should be updated and changed when market conditions, competition, consumer behavior, or business objectives change. Successful strategies should still be evaluated and adapted over time.

One reason accurate sales forecasting is important is that the forecast is the basis for other operational activities — inventory planning, staffing levels, purchasing, and production schedules all flow from the sales forecast.
A component of a project plan that specifies what IS and what ISN'T included in the project — defining its boundaries. Prevents scope creep (unauthorized expansion of project requirements).
A key responsibility of warehouse employees is determining where to store goods between production and consumption — managing the physical storage, organization, and retrieval of inventory.

term: Too Much Inventory = Holding Costs Increase
definition: When a business has too much inventory on hand for an extended period, holding (carrying) costs increase — including storage, insurance, obsolescence risk, and the opportunity cost of capital tied up in unsold goods.
When a business experiences steadily declining sales, one way to control spending is by reducing payroll — cutting labor costs to match the reduced revenue level.

term: Every Outcome = Result of a Process
definition: A fundamental principle of operations: every business outcome (a product, a service, a decision) is the result of a process. Improving outcomes requires identifying and improving the underlying processes.

term: First Aid When Immediate Treatment Essential
definition: An individual should consider administering first aid to an accident victim when immediate treatment is essential — that is, when delaying care would worsen the outcome or endanger life.

Small-business owners develop and use operating procedures so that everyday tasks are performed in a standard, consistent way — regardless of who does them. This ensures quality, efficiency, and repeatability.

term: Business Process Thinking = Fixing Internal Communication
definition: Business process thinking focuses on how work flows through an organization. Example: a company striving to fix its method of internal communications is engaging in business process thinking to improve workflow.

term: Safety Ordinances = Elevators Must Be Inspected
definition: Certain facility components are inspected by authorized officials to ensure safety ordinances are met. Elevators are a primary example — they must be regularly certified for safe operation.

LinkedIn and other professional social networking sites are appropriate for sharing professional accomplishments such as work awards, certifications, and career updates. Not appropriate: personal photos, complaints about current job, personal social updates.

Rules that govern how shared or organizational property may be used. Example: library computers may not be used for video streaming — a use-of-property rule limiting how a shared resource can be accessed.

Innovative management style is more participative than traditional management — it encourages employee input, flexible job descriptions, and a focus on outcomes rather than rigid functions. Managers act more as facilitators than controllers.

Performance standards set specific, measurable targets for employees that align with organizational goals. Example: each sales rep must contact 5 prospects per week to help the company gain 100 new clients.

A management structure where a supervisor manages only a small number of direct reports — because the work is complex, employees are new, or close supervision is required. Contrast: wide span of control (supervisor manages many employees).

A management style that generally encourages creative ways of doing things, solicits employee input, and involves the team in decision-making. Contrast: autocratic (gives specific orders), laissez-faire (hands-off).

An adaptive management strategy that strengthens an organization by bringing employees together AND empowering them as individuals. Encourages sharing knowledge, continuous improvement, and building collective capability.

Laws and regulations that govern the movement of goods across national borders. Key rules: (1) Exports = goods sent OUT of a country. (2) Imports = goods brought IN. (3) Tariffs = taxes on imports, making foreign goods more expensive. (4) Embargoes = complete bans on trade with a specific country. (5) Export controls = rules about what technology/products can be sold abroad (e.g., defense technology). The U.S. Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS) enforces export control laws.

Rules that govern what can be brought into a country and what taxes (customs duties) must be paid on those items. When traveling internationally, you must declare items above a certain value. Businesses importing goods must pay duties and comply with inspections. A customs broker helps businesses navigate complex import/export paperwork.

An agency relationship exists when one person (the agent) is authorized to act on behalf of another (the principal). Example: a real estate agent acts on behalf of a home seller. Key rules: (1) The agent must act in the principal's best interest. (2) The principal is legally responsible for the agent's actions taken within the scope of authority. (3) An agent who exceeds their authority can create personal liability.

The U.S. has two court systems: federal courts (handle federal law, Constitutional issues, and disputes between states) and state courts (handle most business disputes, contracts, and criminal law). Structure: trial courts (evidence presented) → appellate courts (review decisions) → Supreme Court (final authority). The U.S. Supreme Court interprets the Constitution. Most business cases start in trial courts.

Artificial intelligence is creating new legal questions in 2025/2026. Key issues: Who owns AI-generated content — the user or the AI company? (copyright debates ongoing). Can a business be liable if an AI makes a harmful decision? (emerging negligence cases). The EU AI Act (fully enforced 2025) requires companies to disclose high-risk AI use. U.S. businesses operating with AI must be aware of evolving state-level AI transparency laws.

Using digital tools (email, text, chat apps like Slack or Teams) exposes businesses to risk because: (1) Messages can be forwarded, screenshotted, or hacked — losing confidentiality. (2) Written records create legal evidence that can be used in lawsuits. (3) Employees may accidentally share sensitive data. (4) Phishing attacks target employees via email. Best practices: never send confidential data via personal email, use encrypted channels, train employees on email security.

Turning numbers and data into visual formats so audiences understand trends and patterns quickly. Common types: (1) Infographics — combine icons, text, and data into a visual story. (2) Heat maps — use color intensity to show where activity is highest (e.g., website clicks). (3) Bar/line charts — compare data over time. (4) Dynamic model outputs — interactive dashboards (like Excel pivot tables or Tableau). Why they matter: finance professionals use visualizations to present financial reports, sales trends, and budget comparisons to non-technical audiences.

term: Social Media Brand and Organizational Goals (CO:205)
definition: Your personal social media presence affects your employer. A strong professional social media brand (LinkedIn presence, professional posts) can enhance your credibility and your company's reputation. A harmful personal social media presence (offensive posts, sharing company secrets) can damage company image and result in termination. Employees should consider whether every post reflects on their employer.

Different audiences require different writing styles. Examples: (1) Writing to a client vs. a coworker — more formal with client, more casual with colleague. (2) Writing to a teenager vs. a senior executive — adjust vocabulary and examples. (3) Writing for an international audience — avoid slang, idioms, and cultural references that don't translate. Always ask: Who is reading this? What do they already know? What tone will build trust with them?

As a discussion leader, your job is to guide the group — not dominate it. Key skills: (1) Set a clear agenda and purpose at the start. (2) Draw out quieter members by asking direct questions. (3) Keep the discussion on-topic and on schedule. (4) Summarize key points periodically. (5) Ensure all viewpoints are heard before closing. (6) Document decisions and next steps. Contrast: participating in a discussion (following the leader) vs. facilitating (being the leader).

term: AI Writing Tools and Professional Communication (2025)
definition: In 2025/2026, tools like ChatGPT and Copilot are widely used for drafting professional emails, reports, and presentations. Key understanding for DECA: AI can generate a first draft quickly, but humans must review for accuracy, tone, and confidentiality. Sharing proprietary company data with AI tools can create data privacy risks. Many employers are creating AI usage policies for workplace communication.

Ethical CRM means treating customers fairly and honestly in all interactions. Examples of ethical issues in CRM: (1) Collecting customer data without clear consent. (2) Using customer data to manipulate rather than genuinely help. (3) Discriminating against customers based on race, age, or gender. (4) Making false promises to close a sale. Ethical businesses build long-term loyalty; unethical practices result in legal trouble and lost customers.

CRM technology refers to software that helps businesses manage customer interactions and data. Key tools: (1) CRM software (Salesforce, HubSpot) — tracks customer history, purchases, and complaints. (2) Email automation — sends personalized follow-up messages automatically. (3) Chatbots — AI-powered tools that answer customer questions 24/7. (4) Analytics dashboards — identify loyal customers, at-risk accounts, and trends. Technology in CRM allows businesses to personalize service at scale and respond faster to customer needs.

Customers from different cultural backgrounds have different communication preferences. Examples: (1) In some cultures, direct eye contact is respectful; in others, it is seen as aggressive. (2) Negotiating styles vary — some cultures prefer relationship-building before discussing price; others prefer efficiency. (3) Titles and formality differ — some cultures require formal titles (Mr., Dr.); others prefer first names. Businesses must train staff to recognize and adapt to these differences to serve diverse customers well.

In 2025, many businesses use AI tools to enhance customer service. AI chatbots can now handle complex inquiries (not just FAQs). Sentiment analysis tools automatically detect when a customer is frustrated and escalate to a human agent. Personalization engines analyze past behavior to recommend products. For DECA purposes: understand that technology improves efficiency but human empathy remains essential for resolving serious complaints.

Trade alliances are agreements between countries to reduce tariffs and encourage trade. Key alliances to know: (1) USMCA (United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement) — replaced NAFTA in 2020, governs trade in North America. (2) EU (European Union) — 27-country single market with no internal tariffs. (3) ASEAN (Association of Southeast Asian Nations) — promotes trade among 10 Southeast Asian countries. (4) WTO (World Trade Organization) — global rules-based organization that mediates trade disputes. Impact on business: trade alliances lower costs for imported materials and open new export markets.

A country's geographic location directly affects its ability to trade. Factors: (1) Coastal countries (like the U.S., Japan, Germany) have port access = cheaper shipping. (2) Landlocked countries (like Switzerland, Bolivia) face higher transportation costs. (3) Natural resources affect what countries export (Saudi Arabia = oil, Brazil = agricultural products). (4) Climate determines what crops can be grown and exported. (5) Infrastructure (roads, ports, airports) determines how efficiently goods move.

term: Impact of Country History on World Trade (EC:115)
definition: A nation's history shapes its trade relationships and patterns. Examples: (1) Colonial history — countries often maintain strong trade ties with former colonial powers (e.g., many African nations trade heavily with France or the UK). (2) War history — countries that fought wars (e.g., U.S. and Japan, Germany and France) often rebuilt strong economic partnerships post-conflict. (3) Political history — countries with communist histories (Cuba, North Korea) still face trade restrictions. (4) Trade alliances often form among countries with shared histories and values.

The internet and digital tools have transformed global business. Key impacts: (1) Email and video conferencing (Zoom, Teams) eliminate the need for costly international travel for routine meetings. (2) E-commerce (Amazon, Alibaba) allows businesses to sell to customers worldwide without a physical store. (3) Cloud computing enables teams in different countries to collaborate on the same documents in real time. (4) Social media allows global marketing at low cost. Downside: digital communication increases cybersecurity risks and requires managing data privacy across different national laws.

Globalization = the process by which businesses, cultures, and economies become interconnected worldwide. Benefits for businesses: (1) Access to lower-cost labor and materials globally. (2) Larger potential customer base worldwide. (3) Ability to diversify operations across countries (reducing risk). Challenges: (1) Increased competition from foreign companies. (2) Currency exchange rate risk. (3) Cultural and language barriers. (4) Complex regulatory compliance across multiple countries.

In some countries, paying government officials or business partners unofficial payments to win contracts is common — but it is illegal under U.S. law. The Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA) prohibits U.S. companies from bribing foreign officials. Penalties: fines and imprisonment. This matters for international business: a company cannot win a foreign contract by paying a bribe, even if competitors do. Ethical business practice = competing on quality and price, not corruption.

As countries develop economically, their role in world trade changes. Stages: (1) Developing economies — mainly export raw materials (oil, minerals, crops). (2) Emerging economies — begin manufacturing exported goods (clothing, electronics). (3) Advanced economies — export high-value goods and services (technology, finance, healthcare). The U.S. is an advanced economy — we import many manufactured goods from emerging economies (China, Vietnam, India) and export technology, financial services, and agricultural products.

After supply chain disruptions caused by COVID-19 and U.S.-China trade tensions, many American companies are reshoring (bringing manufacturing back to the U.S.) or nearshoring (moving production to Mexico or Canada under USMCA). This trend reduces dependence on distant supply chains. For DECA: understand that USMCA encourages North American production with rules-of-origin requirements (e.g., 75% of auto content must be from North America).

After aggressively raising interest rates from 2022–2023 to combat inflation, the Federal Reserve began cutting rates in late 2024. In 2025, rates remain elevated by historical standards. For businesses: (1) Borrowing costs are still relatively high — loans and mortgages cost more. (2) Savers earn higher returns on savings accounts and CDs than in the 2010s. (3) The Fed's goal is a 'soft landing' — reduce inflation without causing a recession. Key concept: the Fed's target inflation rate is 2%; actual 2025 inflation is near this target.

The 'status quo' means the current way things are done. Leaders who challenge the status quo ask: 'Is there a better way?' and push for improvement rather than accepting things just because 'that's how we've always done it.' Example: a new employee who suggests a more efficient process rather than accepting the old inefficient one. This is a key leadership trait that drives innovation. Caution: challenge constructively with evidence and solutions, not just complaints.

Inspirational leaders motivate others to achieve beyond what they thought possible. Techniques: (1) Share a compelling vision of the future. (2) Connect people's work to a greater purpose ('you're not just entering data — you're helping customers solve real problems'). (3) Celebrate wins, big and small. (4) Model the energy and dedication you want to see. (5) Be genuinely enthusiastic. Inspired employees work harder, stay longer, and produce better results than those who are merely managed.

Ethical leaders don't just tell others to be ethical — they demonstrate it through their own actions. This is called 'walking the talk.' Examples: (1) A manager who admits a mistake rather than covering it up. (2) A CEO who turns down a profitable but harmful business deal. (3) A team leader who gives equal credit to all contributors. Why it matters: employees look to leaders for cues about acceptable behavior. If leaders model ethics, ethical culture spreads throughout the organization.

Effective leaders acknowledge employees' contributions and celebrate their achievements. Methods: (1) Verbal praise — 'Great job on that presentation!' (2) Public recognition — shout-outs in team meetings or company newsletters. (3) Tangible rewards — bonuses, gift cards, extra time off. (4) Career opportunities — recommending high performers for promotions or stretch assignments. Why it matters: recognition satisfies employees' need to feel valued, which increases motivation, loyalty, and productivity.

term: Interpret and Adapt to Business Culture (EI:112)
definition: Every organization has its own culture — unwritten rules about how things are done. New employees must observe and adapt: (1) Is the culture formal or casual? (Dress code, communication style.) (2) Are decisions made by consensus or top-down? (3) Is it okay to challenge a manager's idea, or is deference expected? (4) What are the unspoken values? (Speed, quality, customer-first?) Adapting to culture doesn't mean abandoning your values — it means working effectively within the system while maintaining your ethics.

Tolerance for ambiguity = the ability to function effectively in situations where things are unclear, changing, or uncertain. In business, decisions often must be made without perfect information. People with high tolerance for ambiguity: (1) Don't freeze when they don't have all the answers. (2) Are comfortable with 'it depends' situations. (3) Can make reasonable decisions based on incomplete data. (4) Adapt as new information arrives. This is especially important in entrepreneurship and leadership roles.

Motivation = driving team members to give their best effort. Key approaches: (1) Understand what motivates each person (money, recognition, challenge, flexibility — different for everyone). (2) Set clear, achievable goals. (3) Remove obstacles that prevent people from doing their best. (4) Provide feedback regularly so people know where they stand. (5) Create a positive, supportive environment. Intrinsic motivation (personal satisfaction) is generally more lasting than extrinsic motivation (money/prizes).

Collaboration = working jointly toward a shared goal, combining each person's strengths. Effective collaboration requires: (1) Clearly defined roles so there is no confusion about responsibilities. (2) Open communication — sharing information and ideas freely. (3) Respect for different viewpoints — best solutions often emerge from disagreement. (4) Accountability — everyone does their fair share. (5) Flexibility — willingness to adjust plans when things change. In finance, collaboration often means cross-functional teamwork (finance + marketing + operations).

An achievement orientation means setting high standards for yourself and consistently working to meet or exceed them. Characteristics: (1) Sets ambitious but realistic goals. (2) Continuously improves skills and processes. (3) Persists through obstacles. (4) Measures performance against objective criteria, not just against others. (5) Seeks feedback to improve. This trait is associated with entrepreneurship, sales success, and leadership advancement.

Entrepreneurship is the process of identifying a need in the market and creating a new business or product to fill it, accepting risk in pursuit of profit and innovation. Key characteristics: (1) Value creation — entrepreneurs create goods, services, or jobs that benefit society. (2) Risk-taking — entrepreneurs invest time and money with no guarantee of success. (3) Innovation — most successful entrepreneurs find a new way to solve an old problem. (4) Economic impact — entrepreneurs drive job creation, competition, and economic growth. Small businesses (under 500 employees) account for about half of U.S. employment.

term: Role Requirements of Entrepreneurs and Owners (EN:040)
definition: Entrepreneurs wear many hats, especially in early stages. Core roles: (1) Visionary — sets the direction and purpose of the business. (2) Manager — hires, leads, and organizes people and resources. (3) Salesperson — attracts customers and builds partnerships. (4) Financial planner — manages cash flow, costs, and funding. (5) Problem-solver — handles daily crises and decisions. Successful entrepreneurs recognize their own weaknesses and hire people who complement their skills. You don't have to be good at everything — you need to build a team that is.

term: Small Business Opportunities in International Trade (EN:041)
definition: Small businesses can participate in international trade through: (1) Exporting — selling products abroad (e.g., a small U.S. winery selling wine to Japan). (2) E-commerce — selling globally through platforms like Amazon Global, Etsy, or Shopify. (3) Licensing — allowing foreign companies to produce your product for a fee. (4) Franchising internationally. Resources available to small businesses: U.S. Small Business Administration (SBA) export assistance programs, Export-Import Bank, and local trade centers. Barriers small businesses face: language, cultural differences, currency risk, and complex regulations.

In 2025, AI tools have dramatically lowered the barriers to starting a business. Entrepreneurs can now: (1) Use AI to write business plans, marketing copy, and product descriptions. (2) Use AI-powered tools to build websites without coding. (3) Use AI analytics to identify market opportunities. (4) Automate customer service with chatbots. New challenge: many people can now start similar businesses cheaply, increasing competition. Successful entrepreneurs in 2025 differentiate through personal brand, niche expertise, and unique customer relationships.

term: Legal Responsibilities for Consumer Financial Products (FI:063)
definition: Consumers have legal protections when using financial products. Key laws: (1) Truth in Lending Act (TILA) — lenders must fully disclose APR, fees, and terms before you sign. (2) Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) — gives you the right to see and correct your credit report. (3) CARD Act (Credit Card Act 2009) — protects cardholders from sudden rate increases and hidden fees. (4) Dodd-Frank Act — created the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) to regulate financial products. Consumers should always read the fine print before agreeing to any financial product.

In 2025, almost all financial management is done online. Best practices: (1) Use strong, unique passwords and enable two-factor authentication (2FA). (2) Monitor accounts regularly for unauthorized transactions. (3) Use bank's official app — avoid accessing accounts on public Wi-Fi. (4) Set up transaction alerts so you are notified of every charge. (5) Know how to dispute unauthorized charges (contact bank within 60 days for credit cards). (6) Understand what FDIC insurance covers (up to $250,000 per depositor, per bank).

Estate planning = arranging how your assets will be distributed after your death. Key tools: (1) Will (Last Will and Testament) — legally documents who receives your property. (2) Trust — a legal arrangement where a trustee manages assets for beneficiaries; can avoid probate. (3) Power of Attorney — designates someone to make financial decisions if you become incapacitated. (4) Healthcare Directive / Living Will — specifies your medical wishes. (5) Beneficiary designations on retirement accounts and life insurance (these override your will). Even young people benefit from basic estate planning, especially if they have dependents.

Options for paying for college: (1) Scholarships and grants — free money, does not need to be repaid. (2) Federal student loans (subsidized vs. unsubsidized) — lower interest rates, flexible repayment. (3) Private student loans — higher rates, less flexible; avoid unless necessary. (4) Work-study programs — part-time campus jobs that help cover costs. (5) 529 savings plans — tax-advantaged accounts for education savings. (6) Community college — lower-cost first two years. Key principle: minimize debt; federal loans before private; exhaust free money first. The FAFSA (Free Application for Federal Student Aid) must be filed to access federal aid.

Technology has transformed accounting. Key tools: (1) Accounting software (QuickBooks, Xero, FreshBooks) — automates invoicing, expense tracking, and financial reports. (2) Cloud accounting — access financial data from anywhere; automatic backups. (3) AI-powered bookkeeping — automatically categorizes transactions. (4) Electronic Data Interchange (EDI) — businesses exchange financial documents electronically. (5) Blockchain — creates tamper-proof financial records (emerging use). Benefits: faster, more accurate, and less paper. Accountants now spend less time on data entry and more on analysis and strategy.

Finance professionals must comply with many laws: (1) SEC regulations — govern how publicly traded companies report financial information. (2) Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX) — requires CEOs and CFOs to personally certify the accuracy of financial statements; heavy penalties for fraud. (3) Dodd-Frank Act — regulates banks and financial institutions to prevent another 2008 financial crisis. (4) Anti-money laundering (AML) laws — financial institutions must report suspicious transactions. (5) Insider trading laws — using non-public information to trade stocks is illegal (Civil and criminal penalties).

Keeping debt at a manageable level is essential to financial health. Strategies: (1) Debt avalanche — pay off highest-interest debt first (mathematically optimal). (2) Debt snowball — pay off smallest balances first (psychologically motivating). (3) Debt consolidation — combine multiple debts into one lower-interest loan. (4) Never miss minimum payments — late fees and credit score damage compound quickly. (5) Avoid lifestyle inflation — don't increase spending just because income rises. Warning signs of dangerous debt: spending more than 20% of take-home pay on non-mortgage debt, using credit cards to cover basic expenses.

BNPL services (Affirm, Klarna, Afterpay) allow consumers to split purchases into installments, often with no interest if paid on time. In 2025, BNPL is regulated more strictly — the CFPB now classifies BNPL as a credit product, requiring lenders to disclose terms clearly. For DECA: understand BNPL as a form of consumer credit with risks (easy to overspend, late fees if missed, can hurt credit score if provider reports to bureaus).

In 2025, neobanks (digital-only banks like Chime, Ally, SoFi) are mainstream. They offer: (1) No monthly fees. (2) Higher-than-average savings rates. (3) Instant money transfers. Traditional banks are competing by launching their own digital services. Key understanding: FDIC insurance still applies to deposits at FDIC-insured neobanks. Investment apps (Robinhood, Fidelity, Betterment) have made investing accessible to teens and young adults.

Employee orientation (also called onboarding) is the process of integrating new hires into the organization. A good orientation covers: (1) Company history, mission, and values. (2) Workplace policies (attendance, dress code, safety). (3) Job-specific training and expectations. (4) Introduction to key people and departments. (5) Benefits enrollment (health insurance, 401k). (6) IT setup and access to systems. Why it matters: effective onboarding reduces early turnover, speeds productivity, and helps new employees feel welcome. Research shows employees who experience strong onboarding are more likely to stay long-term.

HR professionals deal with sensitive employee information and make decisions that affect people's careers. Ethical responsibilities: (1) Confidentiality — never share personal employee information inappropriately. (2) Fair hiring — base decisions on qualifications, not personal relationships or bias. (3) Non-discrimination — apply policies consistently regardless of race, gender, age, religion, or disability. (4) Honest references — provide accurate information when giving references. (5) Protect whistleblowers — employees who report violations must be protected from retaliation.

HR technology (HR tech or HRTech) includes software that automates and improves HR processes. Key tools: (1) Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS) — filter and manage job applicants. (2) HRIS (Human Resource Information Systems) — store employee records, payroll, benefits. (3) Learning Management Systems (LMS) — deliver online training. (4) Performance management software — track goals and performance reviews. (5) AI-powered screening — some tools now screen resumes and schedule interviews automatically. Ethical note: AI hiring tools can perpetuate bias if trained on biased historical data.

In 2025, AI is widely used in HR for resume screening, interview scheduling, and even initial video interviews (using AI to analyze facial expressions and word choice). This has raised concerns about fairness and bias. Several U.S. states now require companies to disclose when AI is used in hiring decisions. For DECA: understand both the efficiency benefits and the ethical concerns (privacy, bias, transparency) of AI in HR.

Groupware = software designed for teams to work together. Examples: (1) Google Workspace (Docs, Sheets, Slides) — multiple people edit the same document simultaneously. (2) Microsoft Teams / Slack — team messaging, file sharing, video calls. (3) Asana / Trello / Monday.com — project management and task tracking. (4) Zoom / Microsoft Teams — video conferencing. Key benefits: enables remote work, reduces email overload, tracks project progress. Key skills: knowing when to use a shared document vs. when to use a meeting.

An environmental scan is a systematic process of gathering and analyzing information about external factors that may affect a business. It covers: (1) Political/legal factors (new laws, regulations). (2) Economic factors (interest rates, inflation, employment). (3) Social/cultural trends (consumer preferences, demographics). (4) Technological advances (AI, automation). (5) Environmental/sustainability trends. (6) Competitive landscape. A PEST or PESTLE analysis is a common framework for organizing environmental scan findings. Businesses use scans to identify opportunities and threats before making major strategic decisions.

Ethical information management means collecting, storing, and using data responsibly. Key principles: (1) Consent — only collect data that people have agreed to share. (2) Purpose limitation — use data only for the purpose it was collected. (3) Data minimization — collect only what is necessary. (4) Transparency — tell people what data you have on them. (5) Security — protect data from breaches and unauthorized access. (6) User rights — allow people to access, correct, or delete their data. Laws like GDPR (Europe) and CCPA (California) enforce these principles.

Business records are documents that provide evidence of business transactions and activities. Types: (1) Financial records — income statements, balance sheets, tax returns. (2) Legal records — contracts, licenses, incorporation documents. (3) HR records — employee files, payroll, performance reviews. (4) Customer records — purchase history, contact information, contracts. (5) Operations records — inventory logs, production schedules. Why they matter: businesses are legally required to keep certain records for specified periods (e.g., tax records for 7 years). Records are also critical in lawsuits and audits.

Businesses face several legal issues when managing information: (1) Data privacy laws — GDPR (EU), CCPA (California), and emerging U.S. federal legislation require businesses to protect customer data. (2) Data breach notification — if customer data is stolen, businesses must notify affected customers within a set time (varies by state). (3) Records retention laws — some records must be kept for specific periods (e.g., payroll records for 3+ years). (4) Intellectual property in data — customer lists, databases, and algorithms may be trade secrets. (5) Cybersecurity obligations — many industries (healthcare, finance) have mandatory cybersecurity standards.

Data privacy is one of the biggest legal issues in tech/business in 2025. The U.S. Congress is debating a federal privacy law (APRA — American Privacy Rights Act). Consumers now have stronger rights to know what data companies collect and to opt out. AI systems that use personal data to make decisions (loan approvals, job screening) are under scrutiny for potential discrimination. For DECA: know that collecting customer data without consent is unethical and increasingly illegal.

Operations management is the oversight of all the day-to-day activities that produce a business's goods or services. It includes: (1) Production planning — deciding what to make, when, and how much. (2) Supply chain management — sourcing inputs efficiently. (3) Quality control — ensuring output meets standards. (4) Capacity planning — matching production ability to demand. (5) Process improvement — continuously finding more efficient ways to work. Good operations management reduces costs, improves quality, and ensures products reach customers on time. Operations is the 'engine' of a business.

Policies are rules that guide decision-making (e.g., 'All expenses over $500 require manager approval'). Procedures are step-by-step instructions for completing a task (e.g., how to process a customer refund). Why they matter: (1) Consistency — everyone does things the same way. (2) Compliance — policies ensure legal requirements are met. (3) Training — procedures make it easy to train new employees. (4) Accountability — clear rules make it easier to identify when something goes wrong. Effective policies are written clearly, communicated to all employees, and reviewed regularly.

Taking ownership of your career means actively planning and pursuing growth — not just waiting for opportunities to appear. Key strategies: (1) Set clear career goals (short-term and long-term). (2) Identify skills gaps and seek training to fill them. (3) Build a professional network — connections lead to opportunities. (4) Seek mentors who can provide guidance and open doors. (5) Update your resume and LinkedIn profile regularly. (6) Ask for stretch assignments at work to develop new skills. (7) Pursue professional certifications relevant to your field (e.g., CFA for finance, CPA for accounting).

Your professional image is the impression you make on colleagues, clients, and supervisors. It includes: (1) Appearance — dress appropriately for your workplace culture. When in doubt, dress slightly more formally than required. (2) Communication — speak and write clearly and professionally. (3) Reliability — show up on time, meet deadlines, keep promises. (4) Attitude — stay positive, especially when under pressure. (5) Digital presence — maintain a professional LinkedIn profile; avoid sharing controversial content publicly. (6) Confidentiality — never share sensitive company or client information. First impressions are lasting — but so are patterns of behavior over time.

Risk management is the process of identifying, assessing, and reducing risks that could harm a business. Steps: (1) Identify risks — what could go wrong? (2) Assess risks — how likely is it? How severe would the impact be? (3) Choose a response strategy. (4) Implement controls. (5) Monitor and reassess. Businesses face many types of risk: financial risk, legal/regulatory risk, operational risk, reputational risk, and strategic risk. The goal is not to eliminate all risk — some risk is necessary for reward — but to ensure risks are understood and managed.

The four main risk management strategies: (1) Avoidance — eliminate the activity that creates the risk (e.g., don't sell in a high-risk country). (2) Reduction/Mitigation — take steps to lower the probability or impact (e.g., install smoke detectors to reduce fire damage). (3) Transfer — shift the risk to another party, usually through insurance (e.g., buy liability insurance). (4) Retention/Acceptance — accept the risk because the cost of avoiding it is too high (e.g., self-insure for minor losses). Most businesses use a combination of all four strategies depending on the type and severity of each risk.

Cybersecurity is one of the top business risks in 2025. Major trends: (1) Ransomware attacks — hackers encrypt a company's data and demand payment to unlock it. (2) Phishing — fraudulent emails trick employees into revealing passwords or clicking malicious links. (3) Data breaches — stealing customer data for resale on the dark web. (4) Supply chain attacks — infecting a vendor's software to reach many companies at once. For DECA: cybersecurity is now a core business risk management issue. Businesses must invest in firewalls, employee training, and incident response plans.

ESG (Environmental, Social, and Governance) risks are increasingly recognized as major business risks. Environmental: climate change, floods, wildfires can disrupt operations and supply chains. Social: labor practices, diversity issues, and community relations affect reputation and recruitment. Governance: poor leadership, ethics scandals, and board failures destroy shareholder value. In 2025, many large companies are required to disclose ESG risks to investors. Even small businesses increasingly face ESG scrutiny from customers and partners who want to do business with responsible companies.

Fixed = constant (rent). Variable = changes with volume (materials).
Direct = traceable (materials). Indirect = shared overhead (rent, insurance).
Financial = external, historical. Managerial = internal, future.
Pure = loss only (insurable). Speculative = gain or loss (not insurable).

term: Nominal vs. Real Rate of Return
definition: Nominal = unadjusted. Real = adjusted for inflation.
Elastic = luxury, price-sensitive. Inelastic = necessity, constant demand.
Durable = lasts long (car). Nondurable = consumed quickly (food).
Recession: spending down, unemployment up. Expansion: opposite.
Surplus = exports > imports. Deficit = imports > exports.

term: Letter vs. Spirit of the Law
definition: Letter = literal words. Spirit = intent.
Revolving = credit limit, interest on balance (credit cards). Installment = fixed payments over time (car loan).
Lean = eliminates waste. Six Sigma = reduces defects/variation.
Formal = official channels. Informal = grapevine/casual.
Downward = management to staff. Upward = staff to management.
Primary = inborn, cannot change (age, race, nationality). Secondary = can change over lifetime (education, income, religion, work experience).
Fiat = no intrinsic value, backed by government (paper bills). Commodity = has intrinsic value (gold, silver).
Prevention = stops the loss before it starts (safety training). Reduction = lessens damage after it starts (sprinklers).
Herd = individuals follow others without thinking. Groupthink = group suppresses disagreement to maintain harmony.
